1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	Stating the triple-overlap multiplicativity of the descent datum, however, first
46	requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
47	of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
48	priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
49	transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
50	transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
51	(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
52	scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
53	cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.
54	
55	\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
56	  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
58	  \mathlibok
59	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
60	  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
61	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
62	  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
63	  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
64	  \[
65	    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
66	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
67	    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
68	  \]
69	  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
70	  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
71	  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
72	  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
73	  Mathlib.
74	\end{definition}
75	
76	\begin{lemma}\leanok
77	[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
78	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
79	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport}
80	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
81	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
82	  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
83	  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
84	  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
85	  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
86	  \[
87	    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
88	  \]
89	  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
90	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
91	  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
92	  transition, satisfying
93	  \[
94	    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
95	    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
96	  \]
97	  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
98	  \[
99	    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
100	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
101	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
102	  \]
103	  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
104	  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
105	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
106	  \[
107	    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
108	      \;:\;
109	    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
110	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
111	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
112	  \]
113	  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
114	  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
115	  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
116	  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
117	  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
118	  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
119	  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
120	  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
125	  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
126	  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
127	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
128	  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
129	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
130	  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
131	  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
132	  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
133	  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
134	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
135	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
136	  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
137	  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
138	  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
139	  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
140	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
141	  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
142	  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
143	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
144	  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
145	  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
146	  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
147	  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
148	  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
149	\end{proof}
150	
151	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap endpoint bridges]
152	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src,
154	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid,
155	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt}
156	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
157	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) and fix
158	  indices \(i,j,k\) with triple overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\).
159	  The three transports of \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} have
160	  endpoints pinned down by three scheme-morphism equalities on \(V_{ijk}\), each a
161	  pure consequence of the glue datum's structural identities:
162	  \begin{itemize}
163	    \item (\emph{src}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ f_{ij} = \mathrm{snd} \circ f_{ik}\)
164	      -- the defining pullback condition of \(V_{ijk}\);
165	    \item (\emph{mid}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})
166	      = (t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ f_{jk}\) -- the pullback condition
167	      composed with the glue datum's \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identity;
168	    \item (\emph{tgt}) \((t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ (t_{jk} \circ f_{kj})
169	      = \mathrm{snd} \circ (t_{ik} \circ f_{ki})\) -- the cocycle core, from the
170	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) associativity, the reversed pullback condition, the
171	      \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\) collapse and the glue datum's cocycle identity.
172	  \end{itemize}
173	  These three equalities align the source, middle, and target sheaves of the three
174	  transports so that the cocycle equation (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}
175	  typechecks.
176	\end{lemma}
177	\begin{proof}  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
178	  Each equality is an identity of morphisms in the category of schemes, proved by
179	  rewriting with the glue datum's axioms: \emph{src} is literally
180	  \(\mathrm{pullback.condition}\); \emph{mid} adds the \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)
181	  factorisation \(D.t_{\mathrm{fac}}\,i\,j\,k\); \emph{tgt} chains
182	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at \((j,k,i)\), the reversed pullback
183	  condition, an auxiliary collapse combining \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at
184	  \((k,i,j)\) with \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\), and finally the cocycle identity
185	  \(D.\mathrm{cocycle}\,i\,j\,k\). No sheaf-of-modules data enters; these are
186	  bookkeeping equalities of the underlying scheme cover.
187	\end{proof}
188	
189	% HISTORICAL (abandoned route): an earlier draft built the glued sheaf as a hand-rolled
190	% presheaf of compatible families (decls gluePresheaf / gluePresheafModule / gluePresheafIsSheaf).
191	% That route is abandoned; the gluing is now the equalizer of pushforwards described in the
192	% Construction paragraph of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}, which subsumes it.
193	
194	\begin{definition}
195	
196	\leanok
197	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
198	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
199	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
200	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:modules_pullbackComp,
201	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
202	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
203	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
204	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
205	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
206	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
207	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
208	  charts. Suppose given:
209	  \begin{itemize}
210	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
211	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
212	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
213	      \[
214	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
215	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
216	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
217	      \]
218	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
219	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
220	  \end{itemize}
221	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
222	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
223	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
224	  \((g_{ij})\):
225	  \begin{enumerate}
226	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
227	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
228	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
229	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each common triple
230	      overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the base-change transports
231	      (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three transition
232	      isomorphisms satisfy
233	      \[
234	        \widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
235	          \;=\; \widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j},
236	      \]
237	      where \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\), \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\),
238	      \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are the transports of \(g_{ij}\), \(g_{jk}\),
239	      \(g_{ik}\) to \(V_{ijk}\) through the scheme-level triple transition
240	      \(t'_{ijk}\) and its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\), reassociated by the
241	      pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} so that all three land on
242	      the same sheaf -- the multiplicative, \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle
243	      condition.
244	  \end{enumerate}
245	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
246	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing is well-defined. Out of this
247	  data the construction produces a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
248	  \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\).
249	
250	  \emph{Construction.} The category \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of sheaves of
251	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has all limits, and both pushforward along a morphism and
252	  these limits preserve the sheaf condition; the glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is therefore
253	  built directly as a limit -- an \emph{equalizer of pushforwards} -- rather than through a
254	  hand-built presheaf of compatible families. Concretely, form the two parallel maps of
255	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
256	  \[
257	    \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
258	      \;\;\underset{b}{\overset{a}{\rightrightarrows}}\;\;
259	    \prod_{i,j} (\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}\,\bigl(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\bigr),
260	  \]
261	  where, on the \((i,j)\)-component, leg \(a\) pushes the \(i\)-th factor forward along the
262	  overlap immersion \(f_{ij}\) using the unit of the pullback--pushforward adjunction
263	  together with the pushforward-composition comparison
264	  \((\iota_i)_{*} \cong (\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}\circ f_{ij}^{*}\) (the dual of
265	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), and leg \(b\) pushes the \(j\)-th factor forward and
266	  additionally transports it across the inverse transition isomorphism \((g_{ij})^{-1}\)
267	  and the glue datum's compatibility identity, so that both legs land on the common overlap
268	  sheaf \((\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i)\). The glued sheaf is the
269	  equalizer
270	  \[
271	    \mathcal{M} \;=\; \operatorname{eq}(a,b) \;\hookrightarrow\; \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i,
272	  \]
273	  the sub-sheaf of tuples of pushed-forward chart sections whose two overlap restrictions
274	  agree -- precisely the compatible families, now realised as a categorical limit rather than
275	  a bespoke presheaf. The cocycle conditions (C1)/(C2) on the family \((g_{ij})\) are
276	  \emph{not} consumed in forming this equalizer object: the limit exists for any family of
277	  transition maps. They are instead what make the equalizer descend correctly, and are
278	  exactly the hypotheses pinned down downstream when the restriction isomorphisms
279	  \cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso} are produced. This specialises effective descent
280	  of sheaves of modules to the concrete open cover \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\)
281	  supplied by a scheme glue datum, and is the form in which the Grassmannian's
282	  tautological bundle and quotient are built.
283	
284	  The glued sheaf comes with three further structures, each recorded as its own block
285	  downstream: the \emph{restriction isomorphisms} \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}
286	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps
287	  \cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}; the \emph{characterisation up to unique
288	  isomorphism} by the restriction property \cref{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}; and the
289	  \emph{descent of morphisms} -- a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
290	  commuting with the transition isomorphisms glues to a unique \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to
291	  \mathcal{N}\) with \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ
292	  \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\) \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}.
293	\end{definition}
294	
295	\begin{definition}[Restriction isomorphisms of the glued sheaf]
296	  \label{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrictionIso}
298	  % NOTE: forward declaration (planned work); the \lean{} target is not yet realised.
299	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
300	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
301	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be the glued sheaf of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}. For each
302	  chart index \(i\) there is a \emph{restriction isomorphism} of sheaves of
303	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
304	  \[
305	    \rho_i \;:\; \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{M}_i,
306	  \]
307	  compatible with the transition isomorphisms: over each overlap \(V_{ij}\) the two
308	  restrictions \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\), transported to the common overlap, differ
309	  exactly by \(g_{ij}\).
310	\end{definition}
311	\begin{proof}
312	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
313	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
314	  By the equalizer-of-pushforwards description \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}, a section of
315	  \(\mathcal{M}\) over an open of the form \(\iota_i^{-1}V\), restricted to the chart
316	  \(U_i\), is recovered from and recovers its \(i\)-th component
317	  \(s_i \in \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \cdot)\) -- the \(i\)-th factor of the product
318	  \(\prod_i (\iota_i)_{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) into which the equalizer embeds; the
319	  chart-restriction bridge \cref{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso} identifies the pullback
320	  \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\) with this restriction, and the \(i\)-th projection
321	  \((s_\bullet) \mapsto s_i\) is the isomorphism \(\rho_i\) (with inverse extending a chart
322	  section by the overlap relation).
323	  The overlap compatibility is the defining relation of the compatible families: over
324	  \(V_{ij}\) the components \(s_i, s_j\) are related by \(g_{ij}\), which is precisely the
325	  statement that \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\) differ by \(g_{ij}\) after transport to
326	  \(V_{ij}\); the triple-overlap coherence needed for this to be consistent is (C2),
327	  whose transports are aligned by \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} and
328	  \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}.
329	\end{proof}
330	
331	\begin{lemma}[Uniqueness of the glued sheaf up to canonical isomorphism]
332	  \label{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue_unique}
334	  % NOTE: forward declaration (planned work); the \lean{} target is not yet realised.
335	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
336	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
337	  Suppose \(\mathcal{M}'\) is another sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules equipped with
338	  isomorphisms \(\rho'_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}' \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
339	  compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps. Then there is a unique isomorphism
340	  \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) intertwining the restriction
341	  isomorphisms, i.e.\ with \(\rho'_i \circ \iota_i^{*}(\cdot) = \rho_i\) for every \(i\).
342	\end{lemma}
343	\begin{proof}
344	  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
345	  A morphism of sheaves of modules on \(X\) is determined by, and may be assembled from,
346	  its restrictions to the members of the open cover \(\{\iota_i\}\), agreeing on the
347	  overlaps -- the locality and gluing of sections
348	  (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the internal hom. The
349	  intertwining condition forces the restriction over \(U_i\) to be
350	  \((\rho'_i)^{-1} \circ \rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}'\);
351	  these local isomorphisms agree on each overlap \(V_{ij}\) because both
352	  \((\rho_i)\) and \((\rho'_i)\) are compatible with the \emph{same} transition family
353	  \((g_{ij})\) (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}). By locality they glue to a
354	  unique global isomorphism \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) with the
355	  required property; uniqueness is the separation half of the same gluing.
356	\end{proof}
357	
358	\begin{definition}[Descent of morphisms along the cover]
359	  \label{def:gr_modules_glueHom}
360	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueHom}
361	  % NOTE: forward declaration (planned work); the \lean{} target is not yet realised.
362	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
363	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
364	  Let \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) be two glued data
365	  over the same glue datum \(D\), with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\) and
366	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i, \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i\). Given a family
367	  of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
368	  commuting with the transition isomorphisms on every overlap (i.e.\ \(h_{ij}\) intertwines
369	  the pullbacks of \(\phi_i\) and \(\phi_j\) over \(V_{ij}\)), there is a unique morphism
370	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) with
371	  \[
372	    \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi \;=\; \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i
373	    \qquad \text{for every } i.
374	  \]
375	\end{definition}
376	\begin{proof}
377	  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
378	  On each chart the prescribed identity defines a local morphism
379	  \((\rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i)^{-1} \circ \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i :
380	  \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{N}\). The commutation of the \(\phi_i\)
381	  with the transition isomorphisms \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\), together with the overlap
382	  compatibility of the \(\rho\)-families (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}), makes
383	  these local morphisms agree on every overlap \(V_{ij}\). By the locality and gluing of
384	  sections (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the internal hom they
385	  promote to a unique global morphism \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) restricting
386	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the
387	  cover.
388	\end{proof}
389	
390	\subsection{Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary morphism}
391	\label{sec:grquot_pullback_free}
392	
393	The functor of points (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}) acts on morphisms by pullback, so
394	it needs the pullback of the trivial bundle to be again trivial --
395	\(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}\) -- for an
396	\emph{arbitrary} scheme morphism \(\varphi\). Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison applies
397	only when the underlying site functor \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is \emph{final}; the
398	following three blocks remove that restriction for every morphism, and the resulting
399	pullback machinery preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness.
400	
401	\begin{lemma}\leanok
402	[The opens-pullback functor is final]
403	  \label{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
404	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.opensMap_final}
405	  For any morphism of schemes \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the inverse-image functor on opens
406	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi : \mathrm{Opens}(T) \to \mathrm{Opens}(T')\),
407	  \(U \mapsto \varphi^{-1}U\), is \emph{final}.
408	\end{lemma}
409	
410	\begin{proof}
411	  Finality means that for every open \(V \subseteq T'\) the structured-arrow category of
412	  opens \(U \subseteq T\) with \(V \le \varphi^{-1}U\) is connected. That category is
413	  nonempty and has the terminal object \(U = \top\) -- one always has
414	  \(V \le \varphi^{-1}\top = T'\), and every object admits a unique arrow to \(\top\) --
415	  and a category with a terminal object is connected.
416	\end{proof}
417	
418	\begin{lemma}\leanok
419	[Pullback of a free sheaf is free]
420	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
421	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso}
422	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
423	  For any scheme morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), there is a canonical
424	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{T'}\)-modules
425	  \[
426	    \varphi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\bigr)
427	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
428	    \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}.
429	  \]
430	\end{lemma}
431	
432	\begin{proof}
433	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
434	  Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison produces this isomorphism whenever the site functor
435	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is final; that finality is \cref{lem:gr_opensMap_final},
436	  which holds for every morphism, so the comparison applies unconditionally.
437	\end{proof}
438	
439	\begin{lemma}
440	
441	\leanok
442	[Free-pullback comparison is natural in the base morphism]
443	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_eqToHom}
444	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_eqToHom}
445	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
446	  Let \(\varphi, \psi : T' \to T\) be morphisms with \(\varphi = \psi\) and \(I\) an index
447	  type. The two free-pullback comparisons \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} along \(\varphi\) and
448	  \(\psi\) agree after transporting the differing pullback sources by the canonical
449	  \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\): the composite of that transport with
450	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\psi)\) equals \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi)\).
451	\end{lemma}
452	\begin{proof}
453	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
454	  Substituting the equality \(\varphi = \psi\) collapses the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) to the
455	  identity and the two comparisons coincide.
456	\end{proof}
457	
458	\begin{lemma}
459	
460	\leanok
461	[Iso-level free-pullback cancellation]
462	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso}
463	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso}
464	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
465	  For equal base morphisms \(\varphi = \psi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), the composite
466	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi) \circ \mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\psi)^{-1}\) is the
467	  \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) transport between the (differing) pullback sources of the free sheaf
468	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\). Stated and proved generically in the base morphisms, so its
469	  use never forces evaluation of a concrete immersion; this is the load-bearing replacement
470	  for the cast chain in the self-identity \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id}.
471	\end{lemma}
472	\begin{proof}
473	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
474	  Substituting \(\varphi = \psi\) turns the composite into
475	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi) \circ \mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi)^{-1} =
476	  \mathrm{id}\), which is the \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) of the now-trivial source equality.
477	\end{proof}
478	
479	\begin{lemma}\leanok
480	[Pullback preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness]
481	  \label{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
482	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
483	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
484	  Let \(\varphi : T' \to T\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
485	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules locally free of rank \(d\)
486	  (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}). Then the pullback \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is
487	  locally free of rank \(d\) on \(T'\).
488	\end{lemma}
489	
490	\begin{proof}
491	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
492	  Choose an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(T\) trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\),
493	  \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\). The preimages
494	  \(\{\varphi^{-1}U_i\}\) cover \(T'\). On each member the factorisation
495	  \(\varphi \circ \iota_{\varphi^{-1}U_i} = (\varphi|_{U_i}) \circ \iota_{U_i}\) of the
496	  restricted morphism, together with the pseudofunctor comparison of iterated pullbacks
497	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), identifies
498	  \((\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M})|_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}\) with
499	  \((\varphi|_{U_i})^{*}\mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\); the latter is
500	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}^{\,d}\) by \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. Hence
501	  \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is locally free of rank \(d\).
502	\end{proof}
503	
504	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
505	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
506	
507	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
508	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
509	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
510	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
511	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
512	
513	\begin{definition}\leanok
514	[Global section of the unit module]
515	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
516	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
517	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
518	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
519	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
520	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
521	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
522	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
523	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
524	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
525	  global function.
526	\end{definition}
527	
528	\begin{definition}\leanok
529	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
530	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
531	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
532	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
533	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
534	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
535	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
536	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
537	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
538	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
539	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
540	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
541	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
542	\end{definition}
543	
544	\begin{lemma}\leanok
545	[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
546	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
547	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
548	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
549	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
550	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
551	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
552	\end{lemma}
553	
554	\begin{proof}
555	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
556	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
557	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
558	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
559	\end{proof}
560	
561	\begin{lemma}\leanok
562	[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
563	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
564	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
565	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
566	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the zero function \(0 \in
567	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the zero endomorphism:
568	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\).
569	\end{lemma}
570	
571	\begin{proof}
572	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
573	  Multiplication by \(0\) sends every section to \(0\), so the corresponding
574	  endomorphism of the unit module is the zero map.
575	\end{proof}
576	
577	\begin{lemma}\leanok
578	[Composition of scalar endomorphisms is multiplication]
579	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}
580	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_comp}
581	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
582	  For \(a, b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the scalar endomorphisms compose by
583	  multiplication: \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) \circ \mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) =
584	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a\,b)\).
585	\end{lemma}
586	\begin{proof}
587	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
588	  Under the identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the
589	  scalar endomorphisms act as multiplication by \(a\) and by \(b\); their composite
590	  is multiplication by \(a\,b\), because the section ring is commutative and
591	  restriction maps are ring homomorphisms.
592	\end{proof}
593	
594	\begin{lemma}\leanok
595	[Scalar endomorphism is additive]
596	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
597	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_add}
598	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
599	  For \(a, b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) one has \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a + b) =
600	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) + \mathrm{scalarEnd}(b)\).
601	\end{lemma}
602	\begin{proof}
603	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
604	  Multiplication by \(a + b\) equals multiplication by \(a\) plus multiplication by
605	  \(b\) on every section, since the restriction maps of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) are additive;
606	  under \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) this is the stated additivity.
607	\end{proof}
608	
609	\begin{lemma}
610	
611	\leanok
612	[Scalar endomorphism of a finite sum]
613	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_sum}
614	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_sum}
615	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
616	  For a finite index set \(s\) and a family \(f : \iota \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\),
617	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(\sum_{i \in s} f_i\bigr) = \sum_{i \in s} \mathrm{scalarEnd}(f_i)\).
618	\end{lemma}
619	\begin{proof}
620	  \uses{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
621	  Induction on the finite set \(s\): the empty case is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
622	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}) and the insertion step is additivity
623	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}).
624	\end{proof}
625	
626	\emph{Infrastructure: matrix automorphisms of the free sheaf.} To realise the
627	\(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transitions one must promote an invertible \(d \times d\)
628	matrix of global functions to an \emph{automorphism} of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf
629	\(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\), exactly as the chart quotient \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}
630	realises the universal matrix. The next blocks assemble this matrix-to-morphism
631	functor and record its two structural identities -- that it carries matrix
632	multiplication to composition and the identity matrix to the identity -- which are
633	the algebraic heart of the bundle cocycle.
634	
635	\begin{definition}\leanok
636	[Matrix endomorphism of the free sheaf]
637	  \label{def:gr_matrixEnd}
638	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd}
639	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
640	  Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), and
641	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) a matrix of
642	  global functions. The \emph{matrix endomorphism} \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is the
643	  endomorphism of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) whose
644	  \((p,q)\)-component, under the presentation of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) as the
645	  finite biproduct of \(d\) copies of the unit module \(\mathbf{1}\), is the scalar
646	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{p,q})\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}). It is
647	  assembled from these components by the universal property of the rank-\(d\)
648	  biproduct, exactly as the chart quotient \(u^I\) is assembled
649	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}). This is the substitute for the (non-existent)
650	  Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) endomorphism of a free sheaf'' primitive.
651	\end{definition}
652	
653	\begin{lemma}\leanok
654	[Matrix endomorphism carries multiplication to composition]
655	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
656	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_comp}
657	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
658	  For \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) the matrix
659	  endomorphisms compose according to the matrix product, with the order reversed by
660	  the contravariance of the component indexing:
661	  \[
662	    \mathrm{matrixEnd}(M) \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)
663	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N \cdot M).
664	  \]
665	\end{lemma}
666	\begin{proof}
667	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
668	  The composition of two biproduct-assembled morphisms is again biproduct-assembled,
669	  with \((i,q)\)-component the sum over the middle index \(p\) of the composites of
670	  components, \(\sum_p \mathrm{component}_{i,p} \circ \mathrm{component}_{p,q}\) (the
671	  categorical matrix product over the biproduct). Each composite of scalar
672	  endomorphisms is the scalar endomorphism of the product,
673	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\circ\mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(ab)\)
674	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}), and a finite sum of scalar endomorphisms is the
675	  scalar endomorphism of the sum (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}); hence the \((i,q)\)-component
676	  of the composite is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(\sum_p M_{q,p} N_{p,i}\bigr) =
677	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl((N\cdot M)_{q,i}\bigr)\), which is precisely the
678	  \((i,q)\)-component of \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N\cdot M)\). The reversal of the factor
679	  order is the contravariance of the column/component indexing.
680	\end{proof}
681	
682	\begin{lemma}\leanok
683	[Matrix endomorphism of the identity is the identity]
684	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}
685	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_one}
686	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
687	  The matrix endomorphism of the identity matrix is the identity morphism of
688	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\): \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(1_{d\times d}) =
689	  \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}}\).
690	\end{lemma}
691	\begin{proof}
692	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
693	  The diagonal components are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\)
694	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the off-diagonal components are
695	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the morphism with this
696	  component matrix is the identity of the biproduct.
697	\end{proof}
698	
699	\begin{definition}\leanok
700	[Free-sheaf automorphism of an invertible matrix]
701	  \label{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}
702	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso}
703	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp, lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}
704	  Let \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) be mutually
705	  inverse, \(M N = 1\) and \(N M = 1\). The \emph{matrix automorphism}
706	  \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(M, N)\) is the isomorphism of free sheaves
707	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) with forward map
708	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) and inverse map \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)\); the two
709	  composites are identities by \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp} (turning the products
710	  \(NM\) and \(MN\) into the relevant compositions) and \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}.
711	  Its forward map is \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) by construction
712	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom}).
713	\end{definition}
714	
715	\begin{lemma}\leanok
716	[Forward map of the matrix automorphism]
717	  \label{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom}
718	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso_hom}
719	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEnd}
720	  The forward map of \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(M, N)\) is \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\).
721	\end{lemma}
722	\begin{proof}
723	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEnd}
724	  Immediate from the definition \cref{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}, whose forward field is
725	  literally \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\).
726	\end{proof}
727	
728	\begin{lemma}\leanok
729	[Matrix automorphisms compose multiplicatively]
730	  \label{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul}
731	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso_mul}
732	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
733	  Let \(A, A', B, B'\) be \(d \times d\) matrices of global functions on \(S\) with
734	  \(A A' = A' A = 1\), \(B B' = B' B = 1\), so that \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(A, A')\)
735	  and \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(B, B')\) are automorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\).
736	  Their forward maps compose to the forward map of the product automorphism, with the
737	  order reversed by the component contravariance:
738	  \[
739	    \mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(A, A')_{\mathrm{hom}}
740	      \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(B, B')_{\mathrm{hom}}
741	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(B \cdot A).
742	  \]
743	  Equivalently, composition of matrix automorphisms realises matrix multiplication of
744	  the underlying matrices. This is the linkage that turns the matrix-level cocycle
745	  identity into a composition identity of sheaf-of-module isomorphisms.
746	\end{lemma}
747	\begin{proof}
748	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
749	  By \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom} the two forward maps are
750	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(A)\) and \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(B)\); their composite is
751	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(A)\circ\mathrm{matrixEnd}(B) = \mathrm{matrixEnd}(B\cdot A)\) by
752	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}.
753	\end{proof}
754	
755	\begin{definition}
756	
757	\leanok
758	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
759	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
760	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
761	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
762	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
763	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
764	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
765	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
766	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
767	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
768	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
769	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
770	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
771	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
772	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
773	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
774	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
775	  \[
776	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
777	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
778	  \]
779	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
780	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
781	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
782	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
783	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
784	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
785	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
786	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
787	
788	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
789	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
790	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
791	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
792	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
793	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
794	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
795	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
796	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
797	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
798	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
799	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
800	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
801	  free sheaves'' primitive.
802	\end{definition}
803	
804	\begin{lemma}\leanok
805	[The $I$-columns of the chart quotient are the identity]
806	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
807	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_ιFree}
808	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
809	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
810	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
811	  For each \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), the chart quotient \(u^I\)
812	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) carries the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th basis section of the
813	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) -- where \(\iota_I(k)\) is the \(k\)-th
814	  element of \(I\) in increasing order -- to the \(k\)-th basis section of
815	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\); that is, the \(I\)-indexed columns of \(u^I\) form the
816	  identity:
817	  \[
818	    e_{\iota_I(k)} \,\circ\, u^I \;=\; e_k.
819	  \]
820	\end{lemma}
821	
822	\begin{proof}
823	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
824	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
825	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
826	  By the biproduct presentation of \(u^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
827	  composite \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I\) reads off the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th column of the
828	  component matrix, whose \(p\)-th entry is the scalar endomorphism
829	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)})\). The \(I\)-indexed columns of the
830	  universal matrix \(X^I\), read through the order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong
831	  I\), form the \(d \times d\) identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}),
832	  so \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\). Hence the diagonal entry is
833	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
834	  off-diagonal entries are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
835	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the resulting column is the \(k\)-th coordinate
836	  inclusion, giving \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I = e_k\).
837	\end{proof}
838	
839	\begin{lemma}\leanok
840	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
841	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
842	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
843	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
844	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
845	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
846	\end{lemma}
847	
848	\begin{proof}
849	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
850	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
851	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
852	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
853	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
854	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
855	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
856	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
857	  \[
858	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
859	  \]
860	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
861	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
862	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
863	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
864	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
865	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
866	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
867	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
868	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
869	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
870	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
871	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
872	\end{proof}
873	
874	\section{The \(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transition cocycle}
875	\label{sec:grquot_bundle_cocycle}
876	
877	The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) is
878	obtained by feeding the gluing primitive \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} the per-chart free
879	rank-\(d\) sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\), the bundle transitions
880	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the two module cocycle hypotheses (C1)/(C2) that primitive
881	requires. The matrix cocycle for the Cramer inverses \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) is already in hand --
882	it is the content of the scheme-level cocycle \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} and the minor identities
883	\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities} -- but the gluing primitive consumes its
884	\emph{module-level} incarnation: an honest isomorphism of sheaves of modules together with
885	its self-identity and triple-overlap multiplicativity. The three blocks of this section
886	package exactly that, decoupling the construction of \(\mathcal{U}\) from the remaining
887	matrix-to-module transport bookkeeping. This is the only net-new infrastructure the
888	universal quotient requires beyond the gluing primitive itself.
889	
890	\begin{lemma}
891	
892	\leanok
893	[Self-minor Cramer inverse is the identity]
894	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_self}
895	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_self}
896	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
897	  The Cramer inverse of the self-minor is the identity matrix:
898	  \((X^I_I)^{-1} = 1_{d \times d}\).
899	\end{lemma}
900	\begin{proof}
901	  \uses{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
902	  The self-minor \(X^I_I\) is the identity block
903	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}), and \(1^{-1} = 1\).
904	\end{proof}
905	
906	\begin{definition}\leanok
907	[The bundle transition \(g_{I,J}\)]
908	  \label{def:gr_bundleTransition}
909	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition}
910	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
911	    lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_transition}
912	  Let \(I, J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) be size-\(d\) subsets and let
913	  \(f_{IJ} : U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\) and \(t_{IJ} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\) be
914	  the overlap immersion and transition of the chart cover (\cref{def:gr_transition}). The
915	  \emph{bundle transition} \(g_{I,J}\) is the isomorphism of sheaves of modules on the
916	  overlap \(U^I_J\)
917	  \[
918	    g_{I,J} \;:\;
919	      f_{IJ}^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\bigr)
920	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
921	      (t_{IJ} \circ f_{JI})^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_{U^J}^{\,d}\bigr)
922	  \]
923	  induced by the invertible \(d \times d\) matrix
924	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J)\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
925	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor, which is invertible because
926	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}).
927	
928	  \emph{Realisation.} The transition is built exactly as the chart quotient
929	  \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}: each entry \((X^I_J)^{-1}_{p,q} \in R^I_J\) is a global
930	  function on the overlap, its scalar endomorphism
931	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}_{p,q}\bigr)\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the
932	  \((p,q)\)-component, and the universal property of the rank-\(d\) biproduct assembles
933	  these into an automorphism of the free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,d}\); the
934	  source and target pullbacks of free sheaves are identified with this
935	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,d}\) through the free-pullback comparisons
936	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} along \(f_{IJ}\) and \(t_{IJ} \circ f_{JI}\), conjugating
937	  the matrix automorphism into the displayed isomorphism. Invertibility of the matrix makes
938	  \(g_{I,J}\) an isomorphism.
939	\end{definition}

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{definition}\leanok
117	[Presheaf-of-modules category in monoidal form]
118	  \label{def:monoidalPresheaf}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.MonoidalPresheaf}
120	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
121	  For a scheme \(X\), write \(\PshMod_{\otimes}(X)\) for the category of presheaves
122	  of modules over the underlying presheaf of (plain) rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\),
123	  obtained by composing the structure sheaf with the forgetful functor
124	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat} \to \mathrm{RingCat}\). This is definitionally
125	  \(X.\PshMod\), but presented in the exact form for which Mathlib equips it with
126	  the symmetric monoidal structure of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib};
127	  it is the carrier on which the objectwise tensor product of
128	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} is taken.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\begin{definition}\leanok
132	[Scheme-level module sheafification functor]
133	  \label{def:schemeModuleSheafification}
134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification}
135	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
136	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{module sheafification functor}
137	  \((-)^{\#} : X.\PshMod \to X.\Modules\) is Mathlib's sheafification functor
138	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) specialised to the identity
139	  morphism of the underlying presheaf of rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (a locally
140	  bijective map). Applying it to the objectwise tensor presheaf is what defines the
141	  sheaf tensor product \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}.
142	\end{definition}
143	
144	\begin{definition}\leanok
145	[Unit module of a scheme]
146	  \label{def:unitModule}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitModule}
148	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
149	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{unit module} \(\mathbf{1}_X \in X.\Modules\) is the
150	  structure sheaf viewed as a module over itself, i.e.\ Mathlib's
151	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.unit}\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) for
152	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It is the value of the zeroth tensor power,
153	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}).
154	\end{definition}
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
158	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
160	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
161	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
162	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
163	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
164	  % L2282-L2297).
165	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
166	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
167	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
168	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
169	  %  $$
170	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
171	  %  $$
172	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
173	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
174	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
175	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
176	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
177	  %  $$
178	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
179	  %  =
180	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
181	  %  $$"
182	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
183	  product).}
184	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
185	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
186	  \[
187	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
188	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
189	  \]
190	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
191	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
192	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
193	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
194	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
195	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
196	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
197	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
198	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
199	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
200	\end{definition}
201	
202	\begin{definition}\leanok
203	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
204	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
206	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
207	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
208	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
209	  % L4220-L4228).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
212	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
213	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
214	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
215	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
216	  %  exactly $n$ times."
217	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
218	  powers).}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
220	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
221	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
222	  on \(m\):
223	  \[
224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
225	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
226	    \qquad
227	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
228	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
229	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
230	  \]
231	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
232	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
233	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
234	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
235	  invertible.
236	\end{definition}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}\leanok
239	[Zeroth tensor power is the unit]
240	  \label{lem:tensorPow_zero}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_zero}
242	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:unitModule}
243	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
244	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\), the unit module
245	  (\cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of the recursive definition
246	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{lemma}\leanok
250	[Tensor-power recursion step]
251	  \label{lem:tensorPow_succ}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_succ}
253	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
254	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) and \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) one has
255	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
256	  \mathcal{L}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); this is the successor clause of
257	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{definition}\leanok
261	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
262	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
264	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
265	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
266	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
267	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
268	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
269	  \[
270	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
271	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
272	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
273	  \]
274	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
275	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
276	  \mathcal{F}\).
277	\end{definition}
278	
279	\begin{lemma}\leanok
280	[Zeroth twist]
281	  \label{lem:moduleTensorPow_zero}
282	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow_zero}
283	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule,
284	    def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
285	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
286	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X\)
287	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of
288	  \cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}, holding by definition, and is canonically
289	  isomorphic to \(\mathcal{F}\) via the right unitor
290	  (\cref{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}).
291	\end{lemma}

293	The next four isomorphisms are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal
294	structure on \(X.\Modules\) that descend through sheafification from
295	\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} using only \emph{functoriality} of the
296	sheafification functor (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) and, for the
297	unitors, the reflective counit isomorphism --- no strong monoidality of
298	sheafification is required. They are the structural inputs of the tensor-power
299	comparison \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
300	
301	\begin{definition}\leanok
302	[Counit isomorphism of module sheafification]
303	  \label{def:sheafificationCounitIso}
304	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafificationCounitIso}
305	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}

307	  adjunction (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
308	  \cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) gives a canonical isomorphism
309	  \((\mathcal{G})^{\#} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) of sheaves of modules,

311	  is fully faithful.
312	\end{definition}

314	\begin{definition}\leanok
315	[Left unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
316	  \label{def:tensorObjUnitIso}

318	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
319	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}

331	  \label{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjRightUnitor}
333	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
334	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}

338	  \cref{def:unitModule}), the sheafification of the presheaf right unitor

343	\begin{definition}\leanok
344	[Braiding of the sheaf tensor product]
345	  \label{def:tensorBraiding}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorBraiding}
347	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:schemeModuleSheafification,

358	The strong-monoidality comparison \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
359	below is assembled from three reduction lemmas --- a localization criterion, the
360	local-isomorphism property of the unit, and their corollary --- together with the
361	coequalizer presentation of the relative module-presheaf tensor product, the single
362	Mathlib-absent brick on which the comparison rests.
363	
364	\begin{definition}
365	
366	\leanok
367	[Grothendieck topology on the opens of a scheme]
368	  \label{def:snap_opensTopology}
369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.opensTopology}
370	  For a scheme \(X\) the \emph{opens topology} \(J\) is the canonical Grothendieck
371	  topology on the poset \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)\) of opens of the underlying
372	  topological space, in which a sieve covers an open \(U\) precisely when the opens
373	  it contains cover \(U\). It is the site on which the abelian sheafification, and
374	  the weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of local isomorphisms of abelian-group
375	  presheaves used in \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff} and
376	  \cref{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}, are taken.
377	\end{definition}
378	
379	\begin{lemma}\leanok
380	[Localization criterion for module sheafification]
381	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
382	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
383	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
384	    def:snap_opensTopology}
385	  For a morphism \(f : \mathcal{P} \to \mathcal{Q}\) of presheaves of
386	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the sheafification \((f)^{\#}\)
387	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules if
388	  and only if the underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves lies in the
389	  weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of the opens topology \(J\) on \(X\) (i.e.\ is a
390	  local isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves).
391	\end{lemma}
392	
393	\begin{proof}
394	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
395	  \leanok
396	  Module sheafification (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is the localization
397	  functor at the class \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying
398	  abelian-presheaf morphism lies in \(J.W\), i.e.\ the inverse image of \(J.W\) along
399	  the forgetful functor. The localization criterion for a reflective adjunction
400	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) then gives the stated
401	  equivalence.
402	\end{proof}

404	\begin{lemma}\leanok
405	[The sheafification unit is an abelian local isomorphism]
406	  \label{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
408	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
409	    def:snap_opensTopology}
410	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the underlying
411	  abelian-group presheaf morphism of the sheafification unit
412	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\)
413	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) is the abelian sheafification
414	  unit \(\mathcal{P} \to a(\mathcal{P})\); it therefore lies in the weak-equivalence
415	  class \(J.W\) of the opens topology.
416	\end{lemma}
417	
418	\begin{proof}
419	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
420	  \leanok
421	  The forgetful functor to abelian-group presheaves carries the module
422	  sheafification unit to the unit of the abelian associated-sheaf adjunction, which
423	  is a local isomorphism, hence a member of \(J.W\).
424	\end{proof}

429	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_unit}

432	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})^{\#}\) of the localization unit is an isomorphism of sheaves
433	  of modules. This is the un-whiskered (\(m = 0\)) special case of

441	  \cref{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit} through the localization criterion
442	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}.
443	\end{proof}

445	\begin{definition}
446	
447	\leanok
448	[Abelian-carrier restriction map of a presheaf of modules]
449	  \label{def:snap_objRestrict}
450	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.objRestrict}
451	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) and a morphism
452	  \(f : U \to V\) in \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}}\) (an inclusion of opens
453	  \(V \subseteq U\)), the \emph{abelian-carrier restriction map}
454	  \[
455	    \mathrm{objRestrict}_{\mathcal{P}}(f) :
456	      \mathcal{P}(U) \longrightarrow \mathcal{P}(V)
457	  \]
458	  is the underlying additive map of the presheaf restriction \(\mathcal{P}(f)\),
459	  viewed as a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear map between the carrier abelian groups. It is
460	  the form in which the presheaf restriction enters the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor
461	  presheaves \cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf} and
462	  \cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}; its carriers are pinned to print as
463	  \(\mathcal{P}(U), \mathcal{P}(V)\) so that the tensor-of-restrictions maps unify
464	  on elementary tensors.
465	\end{definition}
466	
467	\begin{lemma}
468	
469	\leanok
470	[Abelian-carrier restriction on elements]
471	  \label{lem:snap_objRestrict_apply}
472	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.objRestrict_apply}
473	  \uses{def:snap_objRestrict}
474	  For a presheaf of modules \(\mathcal{P}\), a morphism \(f\) of opens, and a
475	  section \(x \in \mathcal{P}(U)\), one has
476	  \(\mathrm{objRestrict}_{\mathcal{P}}(f)\,(x) = \mathcal{P}(f)\,(x)\): the
477	  abelian-carrier restriction agrees with the presheaf restriction on elements, by
478	  definition.
479	\end{lemma}
480	
481	\begin{lemma}
482	
483	\leanok
484	[Identity law for the abelian-carrier restriction]
485	  \label{lem:snap_objRestrict_id}
486	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.objRestrict_id}
487	  \uses{def:snap_objRestrict}
488	  For a presheaf of modules \(\mathcal{P}\) and an open \(U\),
489	  \(\mathrm{objRestrict}_{\mathcal{P}}(\mathrm{id}_U) = \mathrm{id}\): restriction
490	  along the identity is the identity, since \(\mathcal{P}\) preserves identities.
491	\end{lemma}
492	
493	\begin{lemma}
494	
495	\leanok
496	[Composition law for the abelian-carrier restriction]
497	  \label{lem:snap_objRestrict_comp}
498	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.objRestrict_comp}
499	  \uses{def:snap_objRestrict}
500	  For a presheaf of modules \(\mathcal{P}\) and composable morphisms \(f : U \to V\),
501	  \(g : V \to W\) of opens, restriction along the composite is the composite of the
502	  restrictions,
503	  \[
504	    \mathrm{objRestrict}_{\mathcal{P}}(f \,;\, g)
505	      = \mathrm{objRestrict}_{\mathcal{P}}(g) \circ
506	        \mathrm{objRestrict}_{\mathcal{P}}(f),
507	  \]
508	  since \(\mathcal{P}\) preserves composition.
509	\end{lemma}
