511	\begin{definition}\leanok
512	[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf]
513	  \label{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
514	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorDomainPresheaf}
515	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib, def:snap_objRestrict,
516	    lem:snap_objRestrict_id, lem:snap_objRestrict_comp}
517	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
518	  assignment \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\),
519	  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the underlying restriction
520	  maps, is a functor \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is
521	  the codomain row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
522	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}); functoriality follows from the
523	  functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) (identity and composition laws
524	  by \(\otimes\)-induction).
525	\end{definition}
526	
527	\begin{definition}\leanok
528	[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor triple presheaf]
529	  \label{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}
530	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorTriplePresheaf}
531	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib, def:snap_objRestrict,
532	    lem:snap_objRestrict_id, lem:snap_objRestrict_comp}
533	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
534	  assignment
535	  \[
536	    U \;\longmapsto\;
537	      \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
538	        \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
539	  \]
540	  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the three underlying
541	  restriction maps --- those of \(\mathcal{P}\), of the structure presheaf
542	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the middle factor restricting along the ring restriction
543	  map), and of \(\mathcal{Q}\) --- is a functor
544	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is the \emph{domain}
545	  row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
546	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}): objectwise it is exactly the
547	  triple tensor \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
548	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) on which the two
549	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps of
550	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} act, its codomain row being
551	  the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf \cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}.
552	  Functoriality (the identity and composition laws of the restriction maps)
553	  follows from the functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) --- the
554	  \(\mathrm{map\_id}\) and \(\mathrm{map\_comp}\) laws --- checked on elementary
555	  tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
556	\end{definition}
557	
558	\begin{definition}\leanok
559	[Left-action natural transformation of the coequalizer]
560	  \label{def:relTensorActL}
561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActL}
562	  \uses{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf,
563	    lem:snap_objRestrict_apply}
564	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
565	  \emph{left-action} natural transformation
566	  \[
567	    a_L : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
568	      (\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U))\bigr)
569	    \;\longrightarrow\;
570	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
571	  \]
572	  from the triple presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the
573	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) is, on an
574	  open \(U\), the additive map
575	  \[
576	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
577	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
578	    \longrightarrow
579	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
580	    \qquad
581	    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto (s \cdot m) \otimes n,
582	  \]
583	  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
584	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{P}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
585	  action map \(\mathrm{actLmap}\) of
586	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}; together with the parallel
587	  right-action transformation it is one of the two rows whose coequalizer
588	  presents the relative tensor presheaf \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
589	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}). Naturality in \(U\) is the
590	  compatibility of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction
591	  of \(s \cdot m\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), so \(a_L\) commutes with the
592	  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps; this is checked on
593	  elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
594	\end{definition}
595	
596	\begin{definition}\leanok
597	[Right-action natural transformation of the coequalizer]
598	  \label{def:relTensorActR}
599	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActR}
600	  \uses{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf,
601	    lem:snap_objRestrict_apply}
602	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
603	  \emph{right-action} natural transformation
604	  \[
605	    a_R : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
606	      (\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U))\bigr)
607	    \;\longrightarrow\;
608	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
609	  \]
610	  from the triple presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the
611	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) is, on an
612	  open \(U\), the additive map
613	  \[
614	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
615	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
616	    \longrightarrow
617	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
618	    \qquad
619	    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n),
620	  \]
621	  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
622	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{Q}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
623	  action map \(\mathrm{actRmap}\) of
624	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}, the mirror of the left-action
625	  transformation \(a_L\) (\cref{def:relTensorActL}); together \(a_L\) and \(a_R\)
626	  form the parallel pair whose coequalizer presents the relative tensor presheaf
627	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\). Naturality in \(U\) is the compatibility
628	  of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction of
629	  \(s \cdot n\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (n|_V)\), so \(a_R\) commutes with the
630	  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps. The verification
631	  reduces to the \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-semilinearity of the restriction maps of
632	  \(\mathcal{Q}\), checked on elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
633	\end{definition}
634	
635	\begin{definition}
636	
637	\leanok
638	[Projection natural transformation of the coequalizer]
639	  \label{def:relTensorProj}
640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorProj}
641	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf, def:snap_objRestrict}
642	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
643	  \emph{projection} natural transformation
644	  \[
645	    \pi : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
646	      \;\longrightarrow\;
647	      \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
648	  \]
649	  from the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) to
650	  the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the presheaf-level monoidal tensor
651	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\) (Mathlib's
652	  \(\mathrm{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\)) is, on an open \(U\), the
653	  canonical quotient additive map
654	  \[
655	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
656	    \longrightarrow
657	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U),
658	    \qquad
659	    m \otimes n \longmapsto m \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} n,
660	  \]
661	  the objectwise projection \(\mathrm{projL}\) of
662	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}. It is the cofork of the
663	  relative-tensor coequalizer: the third leg, the cokernel of \(a_L - a_R\)
664	  (\cref{def:relTensorActL}, \cref{def:relTensorActR}). The apex is identified
665	  with the objectwise value of the presheaf monoidal product through
666	  \cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}.
667	
668	  \emph{Naturality.} The objectwise square asserts
669	  \[
670	    \pi_V \circ
671	      \bigl(\mathrm{restr}_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
672	        \mathrm{restr}_{\mathcal{Q}}\bigr)
673	    \;=\;
674	    \bigl((\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})(f)\bigr) \circ \pi_U
675	    \qquad (f : V \to U),
676	  \]
677	  both composites sending \(m \otimes n\) to
678	  \(\bigl(m|_V\bigr) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(V)} \bigl(n|_V\bigr)\). On elementary
679	  tensors the left-hand side reduces by functoriality of the
680	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of restriction maps followed by the defining formula of
681	  \(\mathrm{projL}\); the right-hand side reduces by \(\mathrm{projL}\) followed
682	  by the objectwise restriction formula for the presheaf monoidal product
683	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}, on elementary tensors:
684	  \((\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})(f)\,(m \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} n) =
685	  (m|_V) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(V)} (n|_V)\)). The two sides then agree by
686	  \(\otimes\)-induction.
687	
688	\end{definition}
689	
690	\begin{lemma}
691	
692	\leanok
693	[Cofork condition of the relative-tensor coequalizer]
694	  \label{lem:snap_relTensorActL_proj_eq}
695	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActL_proj_eq}
696	  \uses{def:relTensorActL, def:relTensorActR, def:relTensorProj,
697	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
698	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
699	  left- and right-action transformations compose equally with the projection,
700	  \[
701	    \pi \circ a_L \;=\; \pi \circ a_R,
702	  \]
703	  as natural transformations from the triple presheaf
704	  (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the underlying abelian-group presheaf of
705	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\), where \(a_L\)
706	  (\cref{def:relTensorActL}), \(a_R\) (\cref{def:relTensorActR}) are the two action
707	  rows and \(\pi\) (\cref{def:relTensorProj}) is the projection. This is the cofork
708	  (coequalizing) condition of the presheaf-level relative-tensor coequalizer
709	  presentation \cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}. It is checked
710	  componentwise: at each open \(U\) both sides agree by the objectwise coequalizing
711	  identity \(\pi_U \circ a_{L,U} = \pi_U \circ a_{R,U}\) of
712	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}.
713	\end{lemma}
714	
715	\begin{lemma}[A \(\mathbb{Z}\)-whiskered stalkwise isomorphism is a local isomorphism]
716	  \label{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}
717	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.ztensor_whisker_localIso}
718	  \uses{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf,
719	    def:snap_opensTopology}
720	  Let \(f : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be a morphism of abelian-group
721	  presheaves on the space \(X\) that is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism} (each
722	  stalk map \(f_x\) is an isomorphism), and let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a further
723	  abelian-group presheaf. Then the whiskered map
724	  \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}\) of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor
725	  presheaves is again a stalkwise isomorphism, hence a local isomorphism (a
726	  member of \(J.W\) for the opens topology \(J\)).
727	\end{lemma}
728	
729	\begin{proof}
730	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
731	  The stalk functor \(\mathcal{A} \mapsto \mathcal{A}_x\) at a point \(x\) is a
732	  filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods of \(x\), and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\)
733	  commutes with filtered colimits, so
734	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C})_x \cong
735	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C}_x\) naturally in \(\mathcal{A}\).
736	  Under this identification the stalk of \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is
737	  \(f_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism since \(f_x\) is.
738	  Thus \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is a stalkwise isomorphism; a
739	  stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves on a space is exactly a local
740	  isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\).
741	\end{proof}

743	\begin{lemma}[Abelian universal property of the relative tensor product]
744	  \label{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
745	  \lean{TensorProduct.liftAddHom}
746	  \mathlibok
747	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Basic}).}
748	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N, P\) be \(S\)-modules (or, more
749	  generally, the abelian-group statement: \(M, N\) modules and \(P\) an additive
750	  commutative group). An additive-bilinear map
751	  \(f : M \to (N \to P)\) that is \emph{\(S\)-balanced}, i.e.\ satisfies
752	  \(f(s \cdot m)(n) = f(m)(s \cdot n)\) for all \(s \in S\), factors uniquely
753	  through the relative tensor product as an additive group homomorphism
754	  \(\widetilde{f} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with
755	  \(\widetilde{f}(m \otimes_S n) = f(m)(n)\). This is the abelian-group universal
756	  property of \(M \otimes_S N\): it is the quotient of \(M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N\)
757	  by the \(S\)-balancing relations.
758	\end{lemma}
759	
760	\begin{lemma}\leanok
761	[The relative tensor of modules is a coequalizer of abelian groups]
762	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
763	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork,
764	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actN,
765	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actM,
766	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap,
767	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap,
768	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL,
769	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_surjective,
770	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_comp_act,
771	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aL,
772	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aR,
773	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor,
774	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_epi,
775	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition,
776	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.cofork,
777	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom,
778	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descMor,
779	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descFac,
780	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap_tmul,
781	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap_tmul,
782	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_tmul,
783	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_apply,
784	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom_tmul}
785	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
786	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N\) be \(S\)-modules. The abelian group
787	  \(M \otimes_S N\) is the coequalizer, \emph{in the category
788	  \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of abelian groups}, of the two parallel
789	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear \(S\)-action maps
790	  \[
791	    a_L,\, a_R :
792	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N)
793	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
794	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N,
795	  \]
796	  where the \emph{left} action map sends \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto
797	  (s \cdot m) \otimes n\) and the \emph{right} action map sends
798	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n)\). The coforking map is
799	  the canonical projection \(\pi : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to M \otimes_S N\),
800	  \(m \otimes n \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
801	\end{lemma}
802	
803	\begin{proof}\leanok  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
804	  Write \(a_N : S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to N\) and
805	  \(a_M : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} S \to M\) for the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear maps
806	  induced by the scalar action (\(s \otimes n \mapsto s \cdot n\),
807	  \(m \otimes s \mapsto s \cdot m\)); the right action map is
808	  \(a_R = \mathrm{id}_M \otimes a_N\) and the left action map is
809	  \(a_L = (a_M \otimes \mathrm{id}_N) \circ \mathrm{assoc}^{-1}\), where
810	  \(\mathrm{assoc}\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor associator. The projection
811	  \(\pi\) is the additive homomorphism produced by the abelian universal property
812	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} from the \(S\)-balanced bilinear map
813	  \((m, n) \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
814	
815	  \emph{Cofork condition.} On an elementary tensor
816	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n)\) we have
817	  \(\pi(a_L(m \otimes s \otimes n)) = (s \cdot m) \otimes_S n
818	  = m \otimes_S (s \cdot n) = \pi(a_R(m \otimes s \otimes n))\), the middle
819	  equality being the defining \(S\)-balancing relation of \(M \otimes_S N\); since
820	  elementary tensors generate, \(\pi \circ a_L = \pi \circ a_R\).
821	
822	  \emph{Universal property.} Given any abelian group \(P\) and a homomorphism
823	  \(g : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to P\) with \(g \circ a_L = g \circ a_R\), the
824	  bilinear map \((m, n) \mapsto g(m \otimes n)\) is \(S\)-balanced (that is exactly
825	  the cofork condition read on elementary tensors), so by
826	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} it factors as a unique
827	  \(\widetilde{g} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi = g\).
828	  Existence is this descended map; uniqueness follows because \(\pi\) is
829	  surjective, hence an epimorphism of abelian groups, so \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi
830	  = g\) determines \(\widetilde{g}\). Packaging existence and uniqueness gives the
831	  colimit cofork.
832	
833	\end{proof}

836	[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]

847	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the

859	
860	\begin{proof}
861	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
862	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
863	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
864	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib,
865	    def:relTensorActL, def:relTensorActR, def:relTensorProj,
866	    lem:snap_relTensorActL_proj_eq}

868	  The argument promotes the objectwise coequalizer of
869	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} to the functor category, in
870	  three steps.
871	
872	  \textbf{1. Objectwise.} For each open \(U\), apply
873	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} with \(S = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
874	  \(M = \mathcal{P}(U)\), \(N = \mathcal{Q}(U)\): the abelian group
875	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is the coequalizer
876	  in \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps
877	  \[
878	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
879	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
880	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
881	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
882	  \]
883	  with cofork map the projection \(\pi_U\).
884	
885	  \textbf{2. Naturality / promotion.} The three objectwise families are precisely
886	  the natural transformations of presheaves on \(X\)
887	  \[
888	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
889	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
890	    \;\overset{a_L,\,a_R}{\rightrightarrows}\;
891	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
892	    \;\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}\;
893	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
894	  \]
895	  built in the previous definitions: the parallel pair is the left- and
896	  right-action transformations \(a_L\) (\cref{def:relTensorActL}) and \(a_R\)
897	  (\cref{def:relTensorActR}) from the triple presheaf
898	  (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf
899	  (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}), and the cofork leg is the projection
900	  \(\pi\) (\cref{def:relTensorProj}) onto the apex. These are morphisms of
901	  functors \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\); naturality of
902	  each in \(U\) is the compatibility of the module action with the restriction
903	  maps of \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\) (the restriction of
904	  \(s \cdot m\) is \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), and likewise for the projection),
905	  recorded in those definitions. Objectwise at each \(U\) the cofork
906	  \((a_L, a_R, \pi)_U\) is the colimit cofork of step 1. Colimits in a functor
907	  category are computed objectwise --- by
908	  \cref{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib} a cocone is a colimit as
909	  soon as every evaluation \((\mathrm{ev}_U)\) of it is --- so the objectwise
910	  coequalizers of step 1 promote to a coequalizer of the displayed parallel pair
911	  \(a_L, a_R\) with cofork \(\pi\) in
912	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\).
913	
914	  \textbf{3. Identification of the apex.} The apex presheaf
915	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the
916	  objectwise tensor formula of the presheaf monoidal structure
917	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}: \((\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p
918	  \mathcal{M}_2)(U) = \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
919	  \mathcal{M}_2(U)\)), exactly the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the
920	  relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
921	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Transporting the colimit of step 2
922	  along this identification exhibits
923	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\), as an abelian-group
924	  presheaf, as the coequalizer of the parallel pair, naturally in \(U\).

927	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise colimits in a functor category]
928	  \label{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib}
929	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Limits.evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits}
930	  \mathlibok
931	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
932	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.FunctorCategory.Basic}).}
933	  Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a category and \(\mathcal{A}\) a category in which the
934	  relevant colimits exist. A cocone over a diagram
935	  \(F : \mathcal{J} \to (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A})\) in the functor category is
936	  a colimit cocone as soon as, for every object \(U\) of \(\mathcal{D}\), its image
937	  under evaluation \(\mathrm{ev}_U : (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A}) \to \mathcal{A}\)
938	  is a colimit cocone in \(\mathcal{A}\). Equivalently, colimits in a functor
939	  category are computed objectwise.
940	\end{lemma}

960	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
961	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
962	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
963	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
964	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
965	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
966	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
967	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
968	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
969	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
970	  the sheafification adjunction
971	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
972	  presheaves of modules, and write
973	  \[
974	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
975	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
976	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
977	  \]
978	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
979	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
980	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
981	  \[
982	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
983	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
984	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
985	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
986	  \]
987	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
988	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
989	\end{lemma}
990	
991	\begin{proof}
992	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
993	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
994	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
995	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
996	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
997	    lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
998	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
999	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
1000	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
1001	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
1002	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
1003	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
1004	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
1005	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
1006	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
1007	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
1008	
1009	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
1010	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
1011	  is the objectwise relative tensor
1012	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
1013	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
1014	  maps
1015	  \[
1016	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
1017	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
1018	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
1019	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
1020	  \]
1021	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
1022	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
1023	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
1024	  pair
1025	  \[
1026	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
1027	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
1028	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
1029	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
1030	  \]
1031	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
1032	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
1033	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
1034	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
1035	
1036	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
1037	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
1038	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
1039	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
1040	  of \(a\)-images. It remains to see that the two whiskered comparison maps
1041	  relating the rows,
1042	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) and
1043	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
1044	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), lie in \(J.W\) (equivalently, become
1045	  isomorphisms after applying \(a\)).

948	  The self-transition is the identity: \(g_{I,I} = \mathrm{id}\) on
949	  \(f_{II}^{*}\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\). This is the module-level form of the descent
950	  condition (C1) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} for the bundle data \((g_{I,J})\).

956	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}), so its Cramer inverse is again the

980	[Cramer-inverse cocycle on the triple overlap]
981	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix}
982	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle_matrix}
983	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,

987	  data to \(V_{IJK}\)) the Cramer inverses of the localised minors satisfy the
988	  multiplicative cocycle identity
989	  \[
990	    (X^J_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}.
991	  \]

996	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,

998	  Write all matrices over the common triple-overlap ring. The image matrix of the
999	  transition \(\theta_{I,J}\) is \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\), and its \(K\)-minor is
1000	  computed by restricting to the \(K\)-indexed columns; since taking a column

1002	  \[

1010	  \]

1012	  via the two-sided Cramer identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}, the

1020	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1021	[Scalar endomorphism is natural under pullback]
1022	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback}
1023	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_pullback}
1024	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1025	  Let \(p : T \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(a \in \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) a
1026	  global function, with comorphism on global sections
1027	  \(p^{\sharp} : \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\).
1028	  Write \(q : p^{*}\mathbf{1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathbf{1}\) for the unit-pullback comparison
1029	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index). Then the pullback of the scalar endomorphism
1030	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\) is conjugate, through \(q\), to the scalar endomorphism of the
1031	  base-changed function:
1032	  \[
1033	    p^{*}\bigl(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\bigr)
1034	      \;=\; q^{-1} \,\circ\, \mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(p^{\sharp} a\bigr) \,\circ\, q
1035	  \]
1036	  as endomorphisms of \(p^{*}\mathbf{1}\); equivalently
1037	  \(q \circ p^{*}(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(p^{\sharp} a) \circ q\). This is the
1038	  single-entry naturality atom underlying the matrix-level statement
1039	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}.
1040	\end{lemma}
1041	\begin{proof}
1042	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1043	  The scalar endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is multiplication
1044	  by the global section \(a\) on the unit module \(\mathbf 1 = \mathcal{O}_S\). Pulling back
1045	  along \(p\) sends multiplication by \(a\) to multiplication by its image, and the comorphism
1046	  \(p^{\sharp}\) is precisely the action of pullback on global functions, so
1047	  \(p^{*}(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a))\) is multiplication by \(p^{\sharp}a\) on \(p^{*}\mathbf 1\). The
1048	  comparison \(q\) is an isomorphism of unit modules intertwining this multiplication on
1049	  \(p^{*}\mathbf 1\) with multiplication by \(p^{\sharp}a\) on \(\mathbf 1\); conjugating back
1050	  along \(q\) therefore yields the claimed identity. The verification is a single computation on
1051	  the unit module, where multiplication by a function commutes with the natural comparison
1052	  \(q\).
1053	\end{proof}

1059	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1060	    def:modules_pullbackComp}
1061	  Let \(p : T \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), and
1062	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\). Let
1063	  \(p^{\sharp}M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T))\) be the entrywise
1064	  image of \(M\) under the comorphism \(p^{\sharp}\), and let
1065	  \(Q : p^{*}(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\) be the free-pullback
1066	  comparison \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at the rank-\(d\) index set. Then the
1067	  pullback of the matrix endomorphism \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is conjugate, through \(Q\), to
1068	  the matrix endomorphism of the base-changed matrix:
1069	  \[
1070	    p^{*}\bigl(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\bigr)
1071	      \;=\; Q^{-1} \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(p^{\sharp}M) \,\circ\, Q .
1072	  \]
1073	\end{lemma}
1074	\begin{proof}
1075	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1076	    def:modules_pullbackComp}
1077	  By \cref{def:gr_matrixEnd}, \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is the biproduct-assembled endomorphism
1078	  of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} = \bigoplus_{k} \mathbf 1\) whose \((k,\ell)\)-component is
1079	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{k,\ell})\). The pullback functor \(p^{*}\) is a left adjoint, hence
1080	  additive and biproduct-preserving; through the coproduct comparison underlying the
1081	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} it carries the biproduct presentation
1082	  of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) to that of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\) and the assembled morphism to the
1083	  morphism reassembled from the pulled-back components, the reassociation of the iterated
1084	  pullbacks being \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. On each single \((k,\ell)\)-component the
1085	  pulled-back scalar endomorphism is, by \cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback}, the
1086	  comparison-conjugate of \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(p^{\sharp}M_{k,\ell}) =
1087	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}((p^{\sharp}M)_{k,\ell})\). Reassembling over the biproduct, the conjugating
1088	  one-element comparisons combine into the single free comparison \(Q\), giving
1089	  \(p^{*}(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)) = Q^{-1}\circ\mathrm{matrixEnd}(p^{\sharp}M)\circ Q\).
1090	\end{proof}

1092	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1093	[Affine global-sections comorphism is the inducing ring homomorphism]
1094	  \label{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_gammaSpec}
1095	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.baseChange_bridge_gammaSpec}
1096	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme}
1097	  Let \(\varphi : A \to B\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
1098	  \(\Spec\varphi : \Spec B \to \Spec A\) the induced morphism of affine schemes. Under the
1099	  natural identification of the global sections of an affine scheme with its coordinate ring
1100	  -- the counit \(\Gamma(\Spec A, \mathcal O) \xrightarrow{\sim} A\) of the
1101	  \(\Gamma \dashv \Spec\) adjunction, and likewise for \(B\) -- the global-sections base-change
1102	  comorphism
1103	  \[
1104	    (\Spec\varphi)^{\sharp} : \Gamma(\Spec A, \mathcal O) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\Spec B, \mathcal O)
1105	  \]
1106	  is identified with \(\varphi\) itself; that is, the square relating
1107	  \((\Spec\varphi)^{\sharp}\) to \(\varphi\) through the two counit isomorphisms commutes.
1108	\end{lemma}
1109	\begin{proof}
1110	  This is the naturality of the counit isomorphism \(\Gamma \circ \Spec \cong \mathrm{id}\) of
1111	  the global-sections / spectrum adjunction: for any ring homomorphism \(\varphi\) the comorphism
1112	  of \(\Spec\varphi\) on global sections is, after transport through the natural counit
1113	  isomorphisms at \(A\) and \(B\), the homomorphism \(\varphi\). No Grassmannian-specific data
1114	  enters; the statement is the affine-scheme naturality square applied to a single ring map.
1115	\end{proof}

1339	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1340	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1341	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
1342	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1343	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
1344	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
1345	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
1346	  %  by putting
1347	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
1348	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1349	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
1350	  \[
1351	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
1352	  \]
1353	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
1354	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
1355	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
1356	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
1357	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
1358	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
1359	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
1360	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore

1362	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
1363	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
1364	  Pl\"ucker embedding.

1374	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
1375	    def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
1376	    def:gr_modules_glueHom}
1377	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1378	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1379	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
1380	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1381	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
1382	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
1383	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
1384	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1385	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
1386	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
1387	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
1388	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
1389	  \[
1390	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
1391	  \]
1392	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
1393	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
1394	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
1395	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
1396	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
1397	  \[
1398	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
1399	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
1400	  \]
1401	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
1402	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
1403	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
1404	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
1405	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
1406	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
1407	\end{definition}
1408	
1409	\section{The functor of points}
1410	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
1411	
1412	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
1413	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
1414	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
1415	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
1416	identities that make that action functorial.
1417	
1418	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
1419	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
1420	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
1421	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
1422	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
1423	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
1424	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
1425	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
1426	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
1427	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
1428	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1429	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1430	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
1431	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
1432	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
1433	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
1434	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
1435	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
1436	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
1437	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
1438	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
1439	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
1440	  functor's value at \(T\).
1441	
1442	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
1443	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
1444	  \[
1445	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
1446	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
1447	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1448	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
1449	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
1450	  \]
1451	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
1452	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
1453	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
1454	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
1455	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
1456	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
1457	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
1458	  map of equivalence classes.
1459	\end{definition}
1460	
1461	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
1462	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
1463	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.
1464	
1465	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1466	[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1467	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1468	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1469	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1470	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
1471	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
1472	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
1473	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
1474	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1475	\end{lemma}
1476	
1477	\begin{proof}
1478	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1479	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
1480	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
1481	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
1482	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
1483	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
1484	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
1485	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
1486	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
1487	\end{proof}
1488	
1489	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1490	[Identity coherence at the free sheaf]
1491	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}
1492	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_id}
1493	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1494	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\) and an index set \(I\), the free-pullback
1495	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1) \cong \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) coincides
1496	  with the component at the free sheaf \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) of the pullback-identity
1497	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1498	\end{lemma}
1499	
1500	\begin{proof}
1501	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1502	  Both sides are cocones on the coproduct \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\); by coproduct
1503	  extensionality it suffices to check equality after precomposing with each coprojection
1504	  \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element case
1505	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}.
1506	\end{proof}
1507	
1508	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1509	[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1510	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1512	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1513	  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
1514	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
1515	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
1516	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
1517	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).
1518	\end{lemma}
1519	
1520	\begin{proof}
1521	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp,
1522	    lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1523	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
1524	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
1525	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
1526	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The compatibility of the mate
1527	  with the adjunction hom-equivalences is supplied by
1528	  \cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}, the reusable conjugate-naturality engine. The
1529	  pentagon coherence of the iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation
1530	  well-defined.
1531	\end{proof}
1532	
1533	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1534	[Mate/conjugate compatibility of the adjunction hom-equivalence]
1535	  \label{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1536	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1537	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2\) be two adjunctions and \(\alpha\) a natural
1538	  transformation between their left adjoints. For a morphism \(f\) the hom-equivalence of
1539	  \(\mathrm{adj}_2\) applied to \(\alpha_c \circ f\) equals the hom-equivalence of
1540	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1\) applied to \(f\), post-composed with the component at \(d\) of the
1541	  conjugate (mate) of \(\alpha\). This is the general naturality of the conjugate
1542	  construction against the two hom-equivalences; it is the reusable engine behind
1543	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
1544	\end{lemma}
1545	
1546	\begin{proof}
1547	  Both sides unfold to the same pasting of unit/counit triangles for the two adjunctions;
1548	  the equality is the standard compatibility of the mate of \(\alpha\) with the
1549	  hom-set bijections.
1550	\end{proof}
1551	
1552	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1553	[Composite coherence at the free sheaf]
1554	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1555	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1556	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1557	  The composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}: for composable \(a, b\) and an
1558	  index set \(I\), the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b \circ a\) at \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\)
1559	  factors through \(a^{*}\) of the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b\), the one for \(a\), and
1560	  the iterated-pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}.
1561	\end{lemma}
1562	
1563	\begin{proof}
1564	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1565	  By coproduct extensionality it suffices to check the identity after precomposing with each
1566	  coprojection \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element
1567	  composite coherence \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} (with the base-ring sheaf
1568	  tracked through each pullback).
1569	\end{proof}
1570	
1571	Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1572	upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
1573	identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
1574	composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
1575	\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
1576	functoriality laws (identity and composition) of the Grassmannian
1577	functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.
1578	
1579	\begin{definition}
1580	
1581	\leanok
1582	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
1583	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
1584	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
1585	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
1586	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1587	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1588	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1589	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
1590	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1591	  % "represents the
1592	  %  contravariant functor
1593	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1594	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1595	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1596	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1597	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
1598	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1599	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
1600	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
1601	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
1602	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
1603	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
1604	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
1605	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
1606	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1607	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
1608	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
1609	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
1610	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
1611	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
1612	  \[
1613	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
1614	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
1615	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
1616	  \]
1617	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
1618	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
1619	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
1620	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
1621	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
1622	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
1623	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
1624	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
1625	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
1626	\end{definition}
1627	
1628	\section{The universal property}
1629	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
1630	
1631	\begin{theorem}
1632	
1633	\leanok
1634	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
1635	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
1636	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
1637	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
1638	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
1639	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1640	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1641	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1642	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
1643	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1644	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
1645	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
1646	  %  contravariant functor
1647	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1648	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1649	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1650	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1651	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
1652	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
1653	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
1654	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1655	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
1656	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
1657	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
1658	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
1659	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
1660	  \[
1661	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
1662	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1663	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
1664	    \qquad
1665	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
1666	      \;\longmapsto\;
1667	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
1668	  \]
1669	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
1670	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1671	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
1672	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
1673	  universal family.
1674	\end{theorem}
1675	
1676	\begin{proof}
1677	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
1678	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
1679	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
1680	    def:grassmannian_functor}
1681	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
1682	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
1683	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
1684	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
1685	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
1686	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
1687	
1688	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
1689	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
1690	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
1691	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
1692	  the composite
1693	  \[
1694	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
1695	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1696	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
1697	  \]
1698	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
1699	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
1700	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
1701	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
1702	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
1703	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
1704	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
1705	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
1706	
1707	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
1708	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
1709	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
1710	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
1711	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
1712	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
1713	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
1714	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
1715	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
1716	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
1717	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
1718	
1719	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
1720	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
1721	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
1722	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
1723	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
1724	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
1725	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
1726	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
1727	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
1728	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
1729	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
1730	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
1731	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
1732	
1733	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
1734	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
1735	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
1736	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
1737	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
1738	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
1739	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
1740	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
1741	
1742	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
1743	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
1744	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1745	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1746	\end{proof}
1747

1047	  \textbf{Whiskered units are stalkwise isomorphisms.} We argue this via stalks
1048	  of abelian-group presheaves (\(\mathrm{TopCat.Presheaf.stalk}\), which exist
1049	  for abelian presheaves on the underlying space, unlike module-sheaf stalks).
1050	  The sheafification unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to a\mathcal{P}\)
1051	  is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism}: for each point \(x\) the stalk map
1052	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x : \mathcal{P}_x \to (a\mathcal{P})_x\) is an
1053	  isomorphism (the defining property of the associated-sheaf unit). The stalk of
1054	  a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of stalks,
1055	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B})_x \cong
1056	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B}_x\), because the stalk is a
1057	  filtered colimit and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) commutes with filtered colimits.
1058	  Under this identification the stalk of
1059	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) at \(x\) is
1060	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism
1061	  (tensoring an isomorphism with the identity). Hence each whiskered unit is a
1062	  stalkwise isomorphism, and a stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves
1063	  on a topological space is a local isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\)
1064	  (\cref{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}). Applying \(a\) therefore turns
1065	  both whiskered comparison maps into isomorphisms.
1066	
1067	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
1068	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
1069	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
1070	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
1071	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
1072	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
1073	\end{proof}
1074	
1075	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
1076	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
1077	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
1078	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1079	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1080	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1081	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
1082	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
1083	  \[
1084	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
1085	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
1086	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
1087	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1088	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1089	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
1090	  \]
1091	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
1092	  arguments.
1093	\end{corollary}
1094	
1095	\begin{proof}
1096	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1097	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1098	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1099	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
1100	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
1101	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
1102	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
1103	  \[
1104	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
1105	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
1106	    \qquad
1107	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
1108	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
1109	  \]
1110	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
1111	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
1112	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
1113	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
1114	  \[
1115	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
1116	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
1117	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1118	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
1119	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
1120	  \]
1121	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
1122	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
1123	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
1124	  morphism
1125	  \[
1126	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
1127	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
1128	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1129	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
1130	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
1131	  \]
1132	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
1133	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
1134	  \[
1135	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
1136	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
1137	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1138	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
1139	  \]
1140	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
1141	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
1142	  composite
1143	  \[
1144	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}}
1145	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
1146	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
1147	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
1148	  \]
1149	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
1150	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
1151	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
1152	\end{proof}

1154	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
1155	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1156	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
1157	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
1158	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
1159	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
1160	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
1161	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1162	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
1163	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1164	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
1165	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
1166	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
1167	  %  (formulation omitted)."
1168	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
1169	  power isomorphisms).}
1170	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
1171	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1172	  \[
1173	    \mu_{m,m'} :
1174	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1175	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
1176	  \]
1177	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
1178	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
1179	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
1180	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1181	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
1182	\end{lemma}
1183	
1184	\begin{proof}
1185	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
1186	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
1187	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1188	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1189	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1190	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
1191	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
1192	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
1193	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
1194	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
1195	
1196	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
1197	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
1198	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
1199	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
1200	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
1201	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
1202	  sheaf tensor products
1203	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
1204	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
1205	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
1206	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
1207	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
1208	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
1209	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
1210	  of the tensor product is never used.
1211	
1212	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
1213	  \(m = 0\),
1214	  \[
1215	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1216	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1217	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1218	  \]
1219	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
1220	  recursive definition
1221	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
1222	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
1223	  \[
1224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1225	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
1226	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1227	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1228	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
1229	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1230	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
1231	  \]
1232	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
1233	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
1234	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
1235	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
1236	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
1237	  \[
1238	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
1239	      \mathcal{L}
1240	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
1241	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
1242	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
1243	  \]
1244	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
1245	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
1246	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
1247	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
1248	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
1249	
1250	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
1251	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
1252	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
1253	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
1254	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
1255	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
1256	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
1257	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
1258	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1259	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
1260	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
1261	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
1262	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
1263	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
1264	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
1265	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
1266	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
1267	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
1268	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
1269	\end{proof}
