627	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
628	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
629	
630	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
631	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
632	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
633	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
634	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
635	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
636	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
637	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
638	
639	\begin{definition}\leanok
640	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
641	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
642	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
643	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
644	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
645	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
646	  \[
647	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
648	  \]
649	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
650	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
651	\end{definition}
652	
653	\begin{lemma}\leanok
654	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
655	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
656	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
657	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
658	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
659	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
660	\end{lemma}
661	\begin{proof}
662	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
663	  \leanok
664	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
665	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
666	\end{proof}
667	
668	\begin{definition}\leanok
669	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
670	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
671	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
672	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
673	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
674	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
675	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
676	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
677	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
678	\end{definition}
679	
680	\begin{lemma}\leanok
681	[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
682	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
683	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
684	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
685	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
686	\end{lemma}
687	\begin{proof}
688	
689	\leanok
690	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
691	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
692	\end{proof}
693	
694	\begin{lemma}\leanok
695	[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
696	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
697	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
698	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
699	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
700	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
701	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
702	\end{lemma}
703	\begin{proof}
704	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
705	  \leanok
706	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
707	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
708	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
709	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
710	\end{proof}
711	
712	\begin{lemma}\leanok
713	[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
714	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
715	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
716	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
717	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
718	  transformed monomial is the radix value
719	  \[
720	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
721	  \]
722	\end{lemma}
723	\begin{proof}
724	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
725	  \leanok
726	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
727	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
728	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
729	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
730	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
731	\end{proof}
732	
733	\begin{lemma}\leanok
734	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
735	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
736	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
737	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
738	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
739	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
740	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
741	\end{lemma}
742	\begin{proof}
743	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
744	  \leanok
745	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
746	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
747	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
748	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
749	\end{proof}
750	
751	\begin{lemma}\leanok
752	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
753	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
754	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
755	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
756	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
757	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
758	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
759	\end{lemma}
760	\begin{proof}
761	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
762	  \leanok
763	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
764	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
765	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
766	\end{proof}
767	
768	\begin{lemma}\leanok
769	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
770	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
771	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
772	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
773	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
774	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
775	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
776	\end{lemma}
777	\begin{proof}
778	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
779	  \leanok
780	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
781	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
782	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
783	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
784	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
785	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
786	\end{proof}
787	
788	\begin{lemma}\leanok
789	[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
790	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
791	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
792	  For any ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and polynomial \(q \in A[X_0, \dots, X_m]\),
793	  the isomorphism \(A[X_0, \dots, X_m] \cong (A[X_1, \dots, X_m])[X_0]\) is natural with
794	  respect to applying \(\varphi\) coefficientwise.
795	\end{lemma}
796	\begin{proof}
797	
798	\leanok
799	  By induction on \(q\): the isomorphism is additive and multiplicative, and agrees with
800	  \(\varphi\) on constants and variables, so it commutes with \(\varphi\) on all polynomials.
801	\end{proof}
802	
803	\begin{lemma}\leanok
804	[Constant inclusion under the variable-splitting isomorphism]
805	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
806	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
807	  For \(s \in R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), the composite of the inclusion
808	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) (sending \(X_j \mapsto X_j\))
809	  with the isomorphism \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) sends \(s\)
810	  to the constant polynomial \(s \in (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\).
811	\end{lemma}
812	\begin{proof}
813	
814	\leanok
815	  By induction on \(s\): a constant and each variable \(X_j\) (\(j \ge 1\)) map to themselves,
816	  and the isomorphism is an algebra map, so it sends products and sums of such to constants in
817	  the outer polynomial ring.
818	\end{proof}
819	
820	\begin{lemma}\leanok
821	[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
822	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
823	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
824	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
825	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
826	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
827	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
828	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
829	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
830	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
831	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
832	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
833	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
834	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
835	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
836	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
837	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
838	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
839	\end{lemma}
840	
841	\begin{proof}
842	
843	\leanok
844	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
845	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
846	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
847	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
848	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
849	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
850	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
851	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
852	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
853	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
854	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
855	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
856	\end{proof}
857	
858	\begin{lemma}[L5b engine anchor — quotient by a monic univariate polynomial is finite]
859	  \label{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite}
860	  \lean{Polynomial.Monic.finite_quotient}
861	  \mathlibok
862	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
863	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(f \in S[X]\) a monic polynomial. Then the quotient
864	  \(S[X]/(f)\) is a module-finite \(S\)-module (indeed free of rank \(\deg f\)).
865	\end{lemma}
866	
867	\begin{lemma}\leanok
868	[L5b.3 — single-variable elimination engine (shared)]
869	  \label{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic}
870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_of_monic_lastVar}
871	  \uses{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
872	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
873	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
874	  % the division algorithm in one variable is the standard mechanism of the support drop.
875	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
876	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
877	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
878	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
879	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (single-variable elimination).}
880	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and \(p \in R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) a
881	  polynomial whose image under the isomorphism
882	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) has a \emph{unit} leading
883	  coefficient — i.e.\ \(p\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to a unit. Then the
884	  quotient \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
885	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) (embedded by \(X_j \mapsto X_j\), \(j = 1, \dots, n\)).
886	\end{lemma}
887	
888	\begin{proof}
889	
890	\leanok
891	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
892	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (single-variable elimination).}
893	  Write \(S := R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) and let
894	  \(\Phi : R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S[X_0]\) be the standard \(R\)-algebra
895	  isomorphism, which sends \(X_0\) to the univariate variable and embeds \(S\) as the constants. Put \(q := \Phi(p) \in S[X_0]\); by hypothesis its leading
896	  coefficient \(u\) is a unit, so the associate \(\tilde q := u^{-1} \cdot q\) is monic and
897	  \((\tilde q) = (q)\) in \(S[X_0]\). The isomorphism \(\Phi\) carries the ideal \((p)\) onto
898	  \((q) = (\tilde q)\), hence descends to a ring isomorphism
899	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p) \cong S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) that is \(S\)-linear, because \(S\) sits
900	  inside both sides as the constants (respectively as the coefficient subring
901	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)). By \cref{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite} the quotient
902	  \(S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) is module-finite over \(S\); transporting finiteness along the
903	  \(S\)-linear isomorphism shows \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over
904	  \(S = R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
905	\end{proof}
906	
907	\begin{lemma}
908	
909	
910	\leanok
911	[L5b — torsion reindex onto fewer variables]
912	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}
913	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_reindex}
914	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
915	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
916	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
917	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
918	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768).
919	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
920	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
921	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
922	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
923	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
924	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
925	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
926	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
927	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
928	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
929	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
930	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
931	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
932	\end{lemma}
933	
934	\begin{proof}
935	
936	\leanok
937	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).

content:
938	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
939	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
940	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
941	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
942	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
943	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
944	
945	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
946	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
947	
948	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
949	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
950	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
951	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
952	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
953	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
954	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
955	
956	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
957	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
958	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
959	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
960	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
961	  \(A_g\).
962	
963	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
964	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
965	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
966	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
967	
968	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
969	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
970	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
971	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
972	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
973	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
974	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
975	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
976	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
977	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
978	
979	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
980	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
981	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
982	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
983	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
984	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
985	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
986	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
987	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
988	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
989	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
990	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
991	
992	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
993	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
994	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
995	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
996	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
997	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
998	
999	  \emph{Module structure and scalar tower.} The conclusion requires \(T_g\) to carry an
1000	  \(A_g\)-module structure compatible with the scalar tower
1001	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). The localisation \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already
1002	  carries the canonical \(A_g\)-module structure induced by localisation, and this is the
1003	  structure required by the scalar tower. The identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must
1004	  therefore be \(A_g\)-linear: the constant-polynomial action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors
1005	  through the original \(A\)-action on \(T\) in exactly the same way as the canonical
1006	  \(g\)-localisation action does, so the two structures agree and the isomorphism is
1007	  \(A_g\)-linear. The \(R\)-module structure and finiteness then pull back from \(T'_g\) to
1008	  \(T_g\) via \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the required scalar tower
1009	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
1010	
1011	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
1012	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
1013	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1014	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1015	\end{proof}
1016	
1017	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
1018	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
1019	
1020	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1021	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1022	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1023	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1024	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
1025	
1026	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1027	[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1028	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1029	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1030	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1031	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1032	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1033	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1034	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1035	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1036	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1037	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1038	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1039	\end{lemma}
1040	\begin{proof}
1041	
1042	\leanok
1043	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1044	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1045	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1046	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1047	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1048	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1049	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1050	\end{proof}
1051	
1052	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1053	[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1054	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1055	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1056	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1057	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1058	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1059	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1060	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1061	\end{lemma}
1062	\begin{proof}
1063	
1064	\leanok
1065	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1066	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1067	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1068	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1069	\end{proof}
1070	
1071	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1072	[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1073	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1074	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1075	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1076	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1077	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1078	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1079	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1080	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1081	\end{lemma}
1082	\begin{proof}
1083	
1084	\leanok
1085	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1086	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1087	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1088	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1089	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1090	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1091	  these conditions gives the claim.
1092	\end{proof}
1093	
1094	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1095	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1096	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1097	  \mathlibok
1098	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1099	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1100	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1101	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1102	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1103	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1104	\end{lemma}
1105	
1106	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1107	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1108	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1109	  \mathlibok
1110	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1111	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1112	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1113	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1114	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).
1115	\end{lemma}
1116	
1117	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1118	[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across an iterated localisation]
1119	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1120	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1121	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1122	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1123	  \(h \in A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) be non-zero. Write
1124	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1125	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h = A_g[h^{-1}]\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1126	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1127	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f = A[f^{-1}]\).
1128	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1129	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1130	\end{lemma}
1131	
1132	\begin{proof}
1133	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1134	  \leanok
1135	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\),
1136	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1137	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1138	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1139	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1140	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1141	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1142	
1143	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1144	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1145	  \((A_g)_h = A_g[h^{-1}]\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1146	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1147	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1148	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(A_{ga} \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1149	
1150	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1151	  \[
1152	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1153	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1154	  \]
1155	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1156	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1157	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1158	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1159	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1160	
1161	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1162	  isomorphism \(A_{ga} \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1163	  \(T_{ga} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1164	  previous step and promoted to an \(A_{ga}\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1165	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{ga}\) is free over
1166	  \(A_{ga}\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1167	\end{proof}
1168	
1169	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1170	[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1171	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1172	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1173	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1174	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1175	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1176	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1177	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1178	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1179	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1180	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1181	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1182	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1183	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1184	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1185	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1186	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1187	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1188	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1189	  core of the induction).}
1190	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1191	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1192	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1193	
1194	\end{lemma}
1195	
1196	\begin{proof}
1197	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1198	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1199	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1200	        \leanok
1201	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1202	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1203	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1204	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1205	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1206	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1207	
1208	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1209	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1210	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1211	
1212	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1213	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1214	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1215	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1216	  \begin{enumerate}
1217	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1218	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1219	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1220	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1221	      \(P_d\)-modules
1222	      \[
1223	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1224	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1225	      \]
1226	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1227	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1228	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1229	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1230	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1231	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1232	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1233	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1234	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1235	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1236	      to \(A_g\).
1237	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1238	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1239	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1240	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1241	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1242	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1243	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1244	      over \(A_f\).
1245	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1246	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1247	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1248	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1249	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1250	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1251	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1252	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1253	      completes the induction.
1254	  \end{enumerate}
1255	\end{proof}

1303	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1304	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1305	
1306	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1307	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1308	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1309	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1310	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1311	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1312	restricted sheaf over the base open.
1313	
1314	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
1315	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1316	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
1317	  \mathlibok
1318	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
1319	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
1320	  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
1321	  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
1322	  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
1323	\end{lemma}
1324	
1325	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1326	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1327	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1329	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1330	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1331	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1332	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1333	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1334	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1335	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1336	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1337	\end{lemma}
1338	
1339	\begin{proof}
1340	
1341	\leanok
1342	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1343	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1344	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1345	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1346	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1347	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1348	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1349	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1350	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1351	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1352	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1353	\end{proof}

1355	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1356	[Half 1 — G1 locality reduction over a finite basic-open cover]

1400	\subsection{Transport engine for generating sections}
1401	
1402	The restriction-of-generators lemma below is assembled from a generic transport
1403	principle: a finite generating family of a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) pushes
1404	forward to a finite generating family of \(F(\mathcal{M})\) along any functor \(F\) of
1405	sheaves of modules that preserves colimits and carries the structure sheaf to the
1406	structure sheaf. The three blocks of this subsection package that principle (the
1407	generating family, its index type, and the finiteness bookkeeping). They are
1408	project-bespoke: Mathlib provides the analogous transport only for full presentations
1409	(with kernel data), not for bare generating families.
1410	
1411	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1412	[Transport of a generating family along a colimit-preserving functor]
1413	  \label{lem:genSections_map}
1414	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map}
1415	  Let \(F : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to \mathrm{Mod}\,S\) be a functor of sheaves of modules
1416	  that preserves colimits and is equipped with an isomorphism
1417	  \(\eta : F(\OO_R) \xrightarrow{\sim} \OO_S\) carrying the unit (structure sheaf) of the
1418	  source to that of the target. Given a generating family \(\sigma\) of \(\mathcal{M}\)
1419	  with index set \(I = \sigma.I\) and epimorphism
1420	  \(\sigma.\pi : \OO_R^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{M}\), the images
1421	  \((\mathrm{mapFree}\,F\,\eta\,I)^{-1} \circ F(\sigma.\pi)\) form a generating family of
1422	  \(F(\mathcal{M})\) with the same index set \(I\). The map is an epimorphism because it
1423	  is the composite of an isomorphism with \(F(\sigma.\pi)\), and \(F\) preserves the
1424	  epimorphism \(\sigma.\pi\).
1425	\end{lemma}
1426	
1427	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1428	[The transported family keeps its index type]
1429	  \label{lem:genSections_map_I}
1430	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_I}
1431	  \uses{lem:genSections_map}
1432	  The index set of the transported generating family of \cref{lem:genSections_map} equals
1433	  the index set of the original: \((\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta)\!.I = \sigma.I\). This
1434	  holds definitionally.
1435	\end{lemma}

1447	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1448	[Half 2, seam 1a — local generators restrict to a smaller open]
1449	  \label{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1450	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1451	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1452	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
1453	  Suppose that on an open \(Y \subseteq X\) the restriction \(\F|_Y\) is
1454	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Then for any open \(V \le Y\) the
1455	  restricted family — the images of those generators under the section-restriction
1456	  \(\Gamma(\F, Y) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) — is again a finite generating family of
1457	  \(\F|_V\). Generation survives passage to the smaller open (a family that generates
1458	  every stalk over \(Y\) generates every stalk over \(V\)), and the index set, and hence
1459	  finiteness, is unchanged.
1460	\end{lemma}
1461	
1462	\begin{proof}
1463	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1464	  \leanok
1465	  A family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_Y\) precisely when the
1466	  induced map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) is surjective on every stalk
1467	  \(\F_y\,(y \in Y)\). Restricting along the inclusion \(V \le Y\) the
1468	  generators map to \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\), and for a point \(x \in V\) the stalk
1469	  \(\F_x\) and the comparison map agree with the value at \(x\) computed over \(Y\), since
1470	  stalks are insensitive to shrinking the ambient open. Hence the induced map
1471	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \to \F|_V\) is surjective on every stalk over \(V\), so
1472	  \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_V\). The index set \(I\) is the same
1473	  finite set, so finiteness is preserved. Concretely the restricted family is obtained by
1474	  transporting the generating family along the (colimit-preserving, unit-preserving)
1475	  restriction functors via the transport engine \cref{lem:genSections_map}, whose index
1476	  type is unchanged (\cref{lem:genSections_map_I}) and which preserves finiteness
1477	  (\cref{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}).
1478	\end{proof}

1480	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1481	[Half 2, seam 1b — finite standard-basic-open subcover of an affine open]
1482	  \label{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1483	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1484	  Let \(W\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), with the canonical identification
1485	  \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\). Given an arbitrary open cover \(\{U_\alpha\}\) of \(W\),
1486	  there is a finite family \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1487	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that each basic open \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\)) is
1488	  contained in some cover member \(U_\alpha\). In particular the \(D(g)\,(g \in t)\) form a
1489	  finite standard-basic-open subcover of \(W\) refining \(\{U_\alpha\}\).
1490	\end{lemma}

1492	\begin{proof}
1493	
1494	\leanok
1495	  Under \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) the basic opens \(D(g)\,(g \in \Gamma(X, W))\) form a
1496	  basis of the topology (\(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}\)). Hence each
1497	  cover member \(U_\alpha\) is a union of basic opens, and the collection of all basic
1498	  opens \(D(g)\) that are contained in some \(U_\alpha\) still covers \(W\). The affine
1499	  scheme \(W\) is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens
1500	  \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already cover \(W\); set
1501	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\), a finite family, each \(D(g_i)\)
1502	  contained in some \(U_\alpha\) by construction. That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1503	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) — i.e. no prime ideal contains all the \(g_i\) — is exactly
1504	  the condition \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\).
1505	\end{proof}

1507	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1508	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1510	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a sheaf of modules on \(Y\). Then
1511	  \(\F\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1512	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1513	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\). No quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is assumed:
1514	  the statement holds for arbitrary sheaves of modules on \(Y\), and applies in particular
1515	  to the restrictions \(\F|_{D(g)}\) arising in the cover assembly.
1516	\end{lemma}

1518	\begin{proof}
1519	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1520	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1521	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1522	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1523	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1524	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1525	  finite generating family.
1526	\end{proof}

1729	[Finiteness transports across a ring isomorphism and a semilinear additive map]
1730	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear}
1731	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear}
1732	  Let \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\) be a ring isomorphism and
1733	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) an additive isomorphism that is \(\sigma\)-semilinear,
1734	  i.e. \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\) for all \(r \in R\), \(m \in M\). If
1735	  \(M\) is a finite \(R\)-module then \(M'\) is a finite \(R'\)-module.
1736	\end{lemma}
1737	
1738	\begin{proof}
1739	
1740	\leanok
1741	  Pick a finite \(R\)-spanning set \(T \subseteq M\). The image \(e(T)\) is finite, and
1742	  it \(R'\)-spans \(M'\): for \(m' \in M'\) write \(m' = e(m)\) with
1743	  \(m = \sum_i r_i t_i\) (\(t_i \in T\)); then
1744	  \(m' = e(\sum_i r_i t_i) = \sum_i \sigma(r_i)\, e(t_i)\) by semilinearity, an
1745	  \(R'\)-combination of \(e(T)\). Hence the top submodule of \(M'\) is finitely
1746	  generated, i.e. \(M'\) is a finite \(R'\)-module.
1747	\end{proof}

1749	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1750	[G1 base case — finite affine sections from a finite generating family]
1751	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1752	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1753	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
1754	        lem:module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear}
1755	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules, and
1756	  \(D \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose the restriction
1757	  \(\F|_D = (\mathrm{pullback}\,D.\iota)^{*}\F\) carries a finite generating family
1758	  \(\sigma\), with index set \(I = \sigma.I\). Then the section module
1759	  \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, D)\). This is the affine base
1760	  case of \(G1\): once the generators are in hand on an affine open, finiteness of
1761	  sections is purely an affine (Spec-side) statement. (Only quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is
1762	  needed — the finite-generation datum is carried by \(\sigma\) directly, so no separate
1763	  finite-type hypothesis on \(\F\) is required.)
1764	\end{lemma}
1765	
1766	\begin{proof}
1767	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
1768	        lem:module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1769	        \leanok
1770	  The content is the transport of the finite generating datum
1771	  from the category of sheaves of modules over the affine open \(D\) to the affine
1772	  scheme \(\Spec \Gamma(X, D)\), where the affine base case applies. Write
1773	  \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\) and use the canonical identification
1774	  \(D \cong \Spec R\) (\(\mathrm{isoSpec}\)). The argument has three steps.
1775	
1776	  \emph{(a) Quasi-coherence on \(\Spec R\).} Transport \(\F|_D\) along
1777	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) to a sheaf of modules \(\F'\) on \(\Spec R\). Finite type of
1778	  \(\F\) is inherited by the restriction, and quasi-coherence of \(\F'\) is the assertion
1779	  that its counit \(\F'.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism; this is the affine
1780	  quasi-coherence criterion \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ} fed by the basic-open
1781	  localization datum \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1782	
1783	  \emph{(b) A finite free epimorphism over \(\Spec R\).} The generating family \(\sigma\)
1784	  furnishes an epimorphism \(\OO_D^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_D\). Transporting along
1785	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) and using that the pullback of the free module along an
1786	  isomorphism is again free (\((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1})^{*}\OO_D^{\oplus I}
1787	  \cong \OO_{\Spec R}^{\oplus I}\)), together with the identification of the free sheaf with
1788	  the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\) of \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) (the associated-sheaf
1789	  functor preserves coproducts and \(\widetilde{R} \cong \OO_{\Spec R}\)), gives an
1790	  epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) with \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) a finite
1791	  \(R\)-module (\(I\) finite).
1792	
1793	  \emph{(c) Identification of section modules.} The affine global-sections functor sends
1794	  \(\F'\) to \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\), which the transport identifies with \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)
1795	  as \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\)-modules.
1796	
1797	  Now the affine base case \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi}, applied to the finite-free
1798	  quasi-coherent epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) over \(\Spec R\),
1799	  shows \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\) is a finite \(R\)-module; by (c) this is \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)
1800	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, D)\).
1801	\end{proof}

2412	        lem:mathlib_isAffineOpen_isoSpec, lem:mathlib_commRingCat_epi_iff_epi}
2413	        \leanok
2414	  The inclusion \(\iota : U \hookrightarrow V\) of opens of \(S\) is an open immersion of
2415	  schemes, hence a monomorphism (\cref{lem:mathlib_isOpenImmersion_mono}). Both \(U\) and
2416	  \(V\) are affine, so under the canonical identifications \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(S, U)\)
2417	  and \(V \cong \Spec \Gamma(S, V)\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_isAffineOpen_isoSpec}) the
2418	  monomorphism \(\iota\) is \(\Spec\) of the restriction ring map
2419	  \(\Gamma(S, V) \to \Gamma(S, U)\). Since \(\Spec\) is fully faithful
2420	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_spec_fullyFaithful}), a monomorphism between affine schemes
2421	  corresponds to an \emph{epimorphism} of section rings; thus
2422	  \(\Gamma(S, V) \to \Gamma(S, U)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{CommRingCat}\). Finally
2423	  \cref{lem:mathlib_commRingCat_epi_iff_epi} repackages this categorical epimorphism as the
2424	  ring-epimorphism property \(\mathrm{Algebra.IsEpi}\,\Gamma(S, V)\,\Gamma(S, U)\).
2425	\end{proof}

2501	[Common basic opens of two affines form a basis of the overlap]
2502	  \label{lem:gf_common_basicOpen_basis}
2503	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_common_basicOpen_basis}
2504	  \uses{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2505	  Let \(X\) be a scheme with affine opens \(W, W_i\) and a point \(x \in W \cap W_i\). Then there
2506	  exist sections \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\) and \(\bar g \in \Gamma(X, W_i)\) with
2507	  \(x \in D(g)\), \(D(g) \le W \sqcap W_i\), \(D(\bar g) \le W \sqcap W_i\), and a common basic
2508	  open \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\). The opens simultaneously basic
2509	  in \(W\) and in \(W_i\) thus form a basis of the overlap.
2510	\end{lemma}
2511	
2512	\begin{proof}
2513	  \uses{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2514	  \leanok
2515	  Pick a basic open \(D(b)\) of \(W_i\) with \(x \in D(b) \subseteq W \cap W_i\)
2516	  (\(\texttt{IsAffineOpen.exists\_basicOpen\_le}\) in chart \(W_i\)), then a basic open \(D(g)\) of
2517	  \(W\) with \(x \in D(g) \subseteq D(b)\) (in chart \(W\)); hence \(D(g) \le W \sqcap W_i\).
2518	  Realise \(D(g)\) back as a basic open of \(W_i\): the restriction \(g|_{D(b)}\) lives in
2519	  \(\Gamma(X, D(b)) \cong (\Gamma(X, W_i))_b\) (\(\texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_basicOpen}\)),
2520	  so write \(g|_{D(b)} = \bar g'/b^{n}\) by \(\texttt{IsLocalization.surj}\) and set
2521	  \(\bar g := b \cdot \bar g'\). Then \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g = D(b) \sqcap X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g' = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g\)
2522	  (\(\texttt{Scheme.basicOpen\_mul}\) plus \(\texttt{basicOpen\_res}\) bookkeeping, since \(b\) is a
2523	  unit on \(D(b)\)), which is the basic-open equality. Note (iter-054 finding): only the basic-open
2524	  equality is achievable in general; a restriction-matched pair \(g|_O = \bar g|_O\) need not exist.
2525	\end{proof}

2531	  \uses{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover, lem:gf_common_basicOpen_basis, lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2532	  % NOTE (iter-054): the "matched pair … that agree over the overlap" (restriction-match
2533	  % g|_O = ḡ|_O) is OVER-SPECIFIED — the Lean conclusion delivers ONLY the basic-open equality
2534	  % `X.basicOpen g = X.basicOpen ḡ` (≤ W ⊓ Wⱼ). Prover finding: the restriction-matched pair is
2535	  % NOT constructible in general (cross-chart realisation only yields ḡ = unit·g on the overlap);
2536	  % the basic-open equality is what B2.2/the assembly consume. Planner: drop the "agree over the
2537	  % overlap" clause here and in the proof sketch. Same correction applies to the (currently
2538	  % unblueprinted) iter-054 helper `gf_common_basicOpen_basis`, which this lemma delegates to.
2539	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open, and \(\{W_j\}_{j}\) a finite family of
2540	  affine opens of \(X\) whose union contains \(W\). Then there is a finite index set \(K\) and, for
2541	  each \(k \in K\), a chart index \(j(k)\) together with sections \(g_k \in \Gamma(X, W)\),
2542	  \(\bar g_k \in \Gamma(X, W_{j(k)})\) cutting out a common basic open
2543	  \[
2544	    D(g_k) \;=\; X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g_k \;\le\; W \sqcap W_{j(k)},
2545	  \]
2546	  such that \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,\{g_k : k \in K\} = \top\) in \(\Gamma(X, W)\) (equivalently, the
2547	  \(D(g_k)\) cover \(W\)). This is the spanning family fed to
2548	  \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span}.

2553	  \leanok
2554	  Inside each overlap \(W \cap W_j\) — open in both affines \(W\) and \(W_j\) — the opens that are
2555	  simultaneously basic in \(W\) and basic in \(W_j\) form a basis of the subspace topology: given
2556	  \(x \in W \cap W_j\), choose \(b \in \Gamma(X, W_j)\) with \(x \in D(b) \subseteq W \cap W_j\) and
2557	  then \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(x \in D(g) \subseteq D(b)\); shrinking \(b\) to the common open
2558	  and recording its restriction over the overlap produces a basic open of \(W\) that is matched, over
2559	  \(W \cap W_j\), by a section \(\bar g \in \Gamma(X, W_j)\) (\cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2560	  certifies \(D(g) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\)). Ranging over all \(j\), and because the \(W_j\)
2561	  cover \(W\), these common basic opens form an open cover of the affine \(W\). The affine \(W\) is

2565	  common basic open contained in some \(W \cap W_{j(k)}\) with matching

2576	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
2577	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
2578	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
2579	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
2580	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
2581	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
2582	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2583	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
2584	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
2585	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
2586	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
2587	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
2588	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
2589	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
2590	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
2591	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
2592	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
2593	
2594	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
2595	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
2596	\end{theorem}
2597	
2598	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
2599	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
2600	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
2601	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
2602	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
2603	% by restricting to a non-empty
2604	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
2605	\begin{proof}
2606	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
2607	        lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover, lem:gf_section_span_flat_descent,
2608	        lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg, lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat,
2609	        lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase, lem:gf_openImmersion_isEpi,
2610	        lem:gf_flat_descend_isEpi, lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span,
2611	        lem:mathlib_flat_of_free, lem:mathlib_free_of_isLocalizedModule,
2612	        lem:gf_flat_isLocalizedModule_sameBase, lem:flat_of_ringEquiv_semilinear,
2613	        lem:flat_localization_models, lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars}
2614	        \leanok
2615	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
2616	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
2617	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
2618	
2619	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact and
2620	  \(U_0\) is affine, hence quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is
2621	  quasi-compact; choose a
2622	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
2623	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
2624	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
2625	
2626	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
2627	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
2628	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
2629	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
2630	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
2631	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
2632	
2633	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
2634	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
2635	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
2636	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
2637	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
2638	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
2639	
2640	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness (source-span descent with base descent along
2641	  \(U \hookrightarrow V\)).} On each patch the localized section module \((M_j)_f\) is free,
2642	  hence flat over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\). We promote these per-patch freeness statements to
2643	  the flatness conclusion for every affine \(U \le V\) and affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\), with
2644	  \(R := \Gamma(S, U)\), by a \emph{source-span} descent on \(W\).
2645	
2646	  Cover \(W\) by basic opens aligned to the patches: by the patch-aligned covering lemma
2647	  \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover} there is a finite family
2648	  \(\{g\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,\{g\} = \top\), each
2649	  \(D(g) \subseteq W \cap W_{j}\) a common basic open matched by a patch element
2650	  \(\bar g \in B_{j}\) (so \(D(g) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\)). For each such \(g\): the
2651	  two-leg transport \cref{lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg} identifies the section module
2652	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) \cong (M_{j})_{\bar g}\). The per-patch freeness gives \((M_{j})_f\)
2653	  free, hence flat over \(A_f\) (\cref{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat},
2654	  \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_free}); since \(D(g)\) lies over \(U \le V = D(f)\) the image of
2655	  \(f\) is already invertible on \(D(\bar g)\), so localizing \((M_{j})_f\) at the
2656	  powers of \(\bar g\) keeps \(A_f\)-flatness
2657	  (\cref{lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}) and yields exactly \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) flat
2658	  over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\).
2659	
2660	  \emph{Base descent along the open immersion.} The inclusion \(U \hookrightarrow V\) of
2661	  affine opens makes \(\Gamma(S, V) \to \Gamma(S, U)\) a ring epimorphism
2662	  (\cref{lem:gf_openImmersion_isEpi}); flat descent along a ring epimorphism
2663	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_descend_isEpi} therefore upgrades ``\(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) flat over
2664	  \(\Gamma(S, V)\)'' to ``\(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) flat over \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\)''. Finally the
2665	  \(\{g\}\) span the unit ideal of \(\Gamma(X, W)\), so the source-span flatness criterion
2666	  \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span}, packaged as
2667	  \cref{lem:gf_section_span_flat_descent}, descends these flat principal localizations to
2668	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) flat over \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\). This holds for every affine \(U \le V\)
2669	  and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\), producing the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over
2670	  \(\OO_V\).

proof_re = re.compile(r'\\begin\{proof\}(.*?)\\end\{proof\}', re.DOTALL)
label_re = re.compile(r'\\label\{([^}]+)\}')

# Find all labeled environments + their following proofs

    body = m.group(2)
    label_match = label_re.search(body)
    if not label_match:
        continue
    label = label_match.group(1)
    has_leanok = '\\leanok' in body
    has_mathlibok = '\\mathlibok' in body
    
    # Check following proof block for leanok

    if proof_m:

    proved = has_leanok or has_mathlibok or proof_leanok
    if not proved:
        print(f'UNPROVED: {label}')
EOF

python3 << 'EOF'
import re

with open('/home/archon/proj/Quot-Foundations/blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex') as f:
    content = f.read()

env_re = re.compile(r'\\begin\{(lemma|theorem|definition|corollary|proposition)\}(.*?)\\end\{(?:lemma|theorem|definition|corollary|proposition)\}', re.DOTALL)
proof_re = re.compile(r'\\begin\{proof\}(.*?)\\end\{proof\}', re.DOTALL)
label_re = re.compile(r'\\label\{([^}]+)\}')

text = content
for m in env_re.finditer(text):
    body = m.group(2)
    label_match = label_re.search(body)
    if not label_match:
        continue
    label = label_match.group(1)
    has_leanok = '\\leanok' in body
    has_mathlibok = '\\mathlibok' in body
    
    # Check next proof block  
    rest = text[m.end():]
    # strip whitespace
    stripped = rest.lstrip()
    if stripped.startswith('\\begin{proof}'):
        proof_m = re.match(r'\\begin\{proof\}(.*?)\\end\{proof\}', stripped, re.DOTALL)
        proof_leanok = proof_m and '\\leanok' in proof_m.group(1)
    else:
        proof_leanok = False
    
    proved = has_leanok or has_mathlibok or proof_leanok
    # show all mathlib entries that aren't proved
    if 'mathlib' in label and not proved:
        print(f'MATHLIB UNPROVED: {label}')
    # Also show items that don't have \lean{} targets
    has_lean_target = '\\lean{' in body
    if not has_lean_target and not proved:
        print(f'NO-LEAN-TARGET UNPROVED: {label}')
EOF

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',

51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

69	% The new third field IS the iter-175+ overlay the prior note flagged: Mathlib
70	% commit `b80f227`'s Stacks-01LL `NatTrans.Coequifibered` predicate is strictly
71	% weaker than the full `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` claimed by Definition
72	% 1.1's prose but is equivalent for the geometric purpose (constructing the
73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence
74	% `AffineZariskiSite.sheafEquiv`. This is the predicate Mathlib's
75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.
78	
79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}
159	
160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\begin{proof}
177	
178	\leanok
179	  The structure morphism is the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
180	  \(\mathcal{A}\); it is the projection underlying \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
181	\end{proof}
182	
183	\begin{theorem}\leanok
184	[Universal property of the relative spectrum]
185	  \label{thm:relative_spec_univ}
186	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}
187	  % NOTE iter-283: removed the spurious `thm:relative_spec_base_change` from this
188	  % \uses. The universal property is the PRIMITIVE statement, proved by reducing
189	  % to the affine-base case and gluing (Lean body of `UniversalProperty` uses
190	  % only `isAffineHom_of_forall_exists_isAffineOpen`/`relativeGluingData`, never
191	  % `base_change`). Base change is a DOWNSTREAM corollary
192	  % (thm:relative_spec_base_change \uses thm:relative_spec_univ), so listing it
193	  % here created a univ <-> base_change dependency cycle that crashed
194	  % `leanblueprint web`.
195	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_affine_base,
196	    def:relspec_structure_morphism}
197	
198	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as the
199	  % structural consequence ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than
200	  % the natural-bijection statement pinned by this prose. Iter-174+ must
201	  % upgrade the Lean type to a ``CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy''
202	  % witness (or equivalent) before any consumer file relies on this theorem.
203	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ continuation: Section ``Relative spectrum as
204	  % a functor'', functor F and Lemma lemma-spec
205	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L427-L466 + L547-L551).
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
207	  % "For any \(f : T \to S\) the pullback
208	  %  \(f^*\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-algebras.
209	  %  We are going to consider pairs \((f : T \to S, \varphi)\) where
210	  %  \(f\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)
211	  %  is a morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-algebras. Note that this is the
212	  %  same as giving a \(f^{-1}\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra homomorphism
213	  %  \(\varphi : f^{-1}\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\), see
214	  %  Sheaves, Lemma REF.
215	  %  This is also the same as giving an \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra map
216	  %  \(\varphi : \mathcal{A} \to f_*\mathcal{O}_T\), see
217	  %  Sheaves, Lemma REF.
218	  %  We will use all three ways of thinking about \(\varphi\),
219	  %  without further mention. [...]
220	  %  In this way we have defined a functor
221	  %  \begin{eqnarray}
222	  %  \label{equation-spec}
223	  %  F : \Sch^{opp} & \longrightarrow & \textit{Sets} \\
224	  %  T & \longmapsto & F(T) = \{\text{pairs }(f, \varphi) \text{ as above}\}
225	  %  \nonumber
226	  %  \end{eqnarray}
227	  %  [...]
228	  %  The functor \(F\) is representable by a scheme."
229	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-spec + section-spec
230	  functor description); [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
231	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
232	  Then \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor
233	  \(F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\),
234	  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;
235	  \varphi : f^* \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T \text{ an } \mathcal{O}_T\text{-algebra map}\}.$
236	  Equivalently, for any \(X\)-scheme \(g : T \to X\),
237	  there is a natural bijection
238	  \[
239	    \Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \;\;\cong\;\;
240	    \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_* \mathcal{O}_T)
241	  \]
242	  (using the adjunction between \(g_*\) and \(g^*\) to convert between
243	  \(\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T\) and \(g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)).
244	  The bijection is natural in \(T\).
245	\end{theorem}
246

248	% (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L553-L599).
249	% "We are going to use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-glue-functors}.
250	%  First we check that $F$ satisfies the sheaf property for the
251	%  Zariski topology. Namely, suppose that $T$ is a scheme,
252	%  that $T = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ is an open covering,
253	%  and that $(f_i, \varphi_i) \in F(U_i)$ such that
